dangerZone.headline: If someone can pretend to be your user, nothing else in your app matters
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: Login looks simple — an email field, a password field, a button. But behind that button is a whole world of things that need to go right: remembering who's logged in across page refreshes, making sure "forgot password" links can't be guessed, stopping someone from trying a million passwords, and handling what happens when people sign in with Google or GitHub. Every shortcut here is a door left unlocked.
dangerZone.failureScenario: You launch your app. 500 people sign up. Everything works great for a few months. Then someone figures out that your "stay logged in" tokens never expire — so a token leaked in a browser extension or shared computer lets them into someone else's account permanently. Now you're emailing 500 users about a security breach before you've even made revenue.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: 6-18 months before someone finds and exploits a weakness
commonMistakes: Passwords stored in a way that's easy to reverse (like a simple scramble instead of a proper one-way lock)
"Stay logged in" tokens that never expire — so a leaked token works forever
"Forgot password" links that follow a guessable pattern
No limit on login attempts — someone can try thousands of passwords per minute
Sign in with Google/GitHub that doesn't properly verify the response, letting attackers fake it

service.title: Is your login service set up correctly?
service.prompt: Look at how we've connected our login service (like Clerk, Auth0, or Supabase Auth). Check these things: Do login sessions expire after a reasonable time? When the service sends us updates (webhooks), do we verify they're actually from the service? Are the URLs where users get redirected after login locked down to only our site? Is the login service's code library up to date? Are we using the service's built-in protections or bypassing them anywhere?
service.whyItMatters: Even when you use a login service, a bad setup can leave holes. It's like having a great lock on your door but leaving the window open.

service.title: Can people access pages they shouldn't?
service.prompt: Check every page and API endpoint that should require login. Are they actually checking if the user is logged in on the server (not just hiding the link in the menu)? Could someone access another user's data by changing a number in the URL? Are admin-only actions actually restricted, or just hidden from the UI?
service.whyItMatters: The most common mistake with login services is hiding buttons in the UI but not actually locking down the pages behind them. It's like removing the doorknob but not the door.

service.title: What happens when someone forgets their password?
service.prompt: Check our password reset setup. Do reset links expire quickly (within an hour)? Does the account lock after too many wrong password attempts? Is email verification required before someone can access the app? When someone types a wrong email on the login page, does the error message reveal whether that email has an account?
service.whyItMatters: "Forgot password" is the back door to every account. If someone can guess or intercept a reset link, the actual password doesn't matter.

service.title: Is login slowing down your app?
service.prompt: Check how our login system affects page speed. Is the app calling the login service on every single page load? Is it caching (remembering) login status so it doesn't need to re-check every time? When someone navigates around the app, does each page wait for a login check before showing anything?
service.whyItMatters: Login checks happen on every page. If each check takes an extra half-second because it's calling an external service, your whole app feels sluggish.

diy.title: Are passwords stored safely?
diy.prompt: Look at how our app stores passwords and handles login sessions. Are passwords scrambled using a strong method (like bcrypt or argon2, not just MD5 or basic SHA)? Do "stay logged in" tokens expire and get refreshed? Is there a limit on how many times someone can try to log in before getting temporarily blocked? Show me exactly where in the code each of these happens.
diy.whyItMatters: If someone gets access to your database, properly scrambled passwords are nearly impossible to reverse. Weakly scrambled ones can be cracked in minutes.

diy.title: Is "Sign in with Google" secure?
diy.prompt: Review our Google/GitHub/social login setup. When someone clicks "Sign in with Google", does our code verify the response is actually from Google (not faked)? Are redirect URLs locked to our domain only? What happens if someone signs up with email and later tries to sign in with Google using the same email — does it merge correctly or create a duplicate account?
diy.whyItMatters: Social login has a lot of back-and-forth between your app and Google/GitHub. If any step isn't verified, someone can fake the process and log in as anyone.

diy.title: Can "forgot password" be exploited?
diy.prompt: Check our password reset flow. Are reset links random enough that they can't be guessed? Do they expire quickly (within an hour)? Can each link only be used once? Is there a limit on how many reset emails someone can request? When a reset fails, does the error message give away whether the email has an account?
diy.whyItMatters: Password reset is the back door to every account. If reset links follow a pattern or don't expire, someone can take over any account.

diy.title: Can your login handle traffic?
diy.prompt: Check how our login system performs under load. How long does the password check take per request? How quickly can it look up session data? If 100 people try to log in at the same time, what happens? Does every page load require a database check to verify the user is logged in?
diy.whyItMatters: Login runs on every single page. If it takes even a fraction of a second too long, multiply that by every user on every page — it adds up fast.

smartMove.reasoning: Login is the riskiest thing to build yourself. One mistake exposes every user. Services like Clerk or Auth0 handle security updates, edge cases, and compliance across millions of apps — and most have generous free tiers. Unless you have a very specific reason to build your own, don't.

services.name: Clerk
services.description: Plug-and-play login pages, Google/GitHub sign-in, and user management — works great with Next.js

services.name: Auth0
services.description: Enterprise-grade login with 30+ social sign-in options and compliance certifications

services.name: Supabase Auth
services.description: Good choice if you already use Supabase for your database — login is built in

smartMove.tradeoffs: You're trusting a third party with your most critical system. If you ever need to switch services, it means rebuilding your entire login flow and migrating all user accounts. Worth it for the security you get in return.

service.item: Accounts lock after several wrong password attempts
Users get automatically logged out after a reasonable period (not left logged in forever)
Protected pages check login on the server, not just by hiding the link
"Sign in with Google" redirect URLs point only to your site (no wildcards)
Webhook endpoints (notifications from your auth service) verify they're really from the service
Password reset links expire within an hour
The login page doesn't reveal whether an email address has an account
Your auth service's code library is on a recent version
Your app still works (gracefully) if the auth service goes down temporarily
Login checks don't noticeably slow down page loads

diy.item: Passwords are scrambled with a strong method (bcrypt or argon2) — not MD5 or plain SHA
"Stay logged in" tokens are long, random, and impossible to guess
Users get a new token when they change their password or gain new permissions
Login attempts are limited (e.g., 10 tries per minute) to prevent password guessing
Password reset links expire within an hour and can only be used once
Error messages don't reveal whether a specific email has an account
Forms that change account state (login, password change) are protected against cross-site request forgery
Checking if someone is logged in doesn't noticeably slow down page loads
Failed login attempts are recorded with timestamps for investigation
The app shows a helpful error (not a crash) if the login system is temporarily unavailable

didYouKnow.stat: 86% of web application breaches involve stolen login credentials, and it takes an average of 287 days to even detect that it happened.

dangerZone.headline: A billing bug doesn't just lose data — it loses real money and real trust at the same time
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: Adding payments looks like dropping in a Stripe widget. But behind that widget is a maze of things that need to work perfectly: what happens when someone upgrades mid-month, how to retry failed cards without annoying people, calculating tax for different countries, handling refunds and disputes, and making sure your app and your payment provider agree on who's paid and who hasn't. Every billing bug is either lost revenue or an angry customer.
dangerZone.failureScenario: You launch a SaaS with monthly billing. Everything's great for a few months. Then a customer downgrades their plan mid-cycle and gets charged full price anyway. You never built the logic for partial refunds. Now you have 200 support emails, a public complaint thread, and your payment provider is flagging you for too many disputes.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: 1-3 months before the first billing dispute becomes a real problem
commonMistakes: Processing the same payment notification twice, causing double charges
No logic for what happens when someone changes plan mid-month (they get overcharged)
Not tagging payments with unique IDs, so retries create duplicate charges
Accidentally logging credit card numbers or payment secrets in error reports
When a card fails, immediately canceling instead of retrying a few times over a few weeks

service.title: Are payment notifications handled correctly?
service.prompt: Look at how we handle notifications (webhooks) from our payment provider like Stripe or Paddle. Check: Do we verify each notification is actually from the provider (not faked)? If the same notification arrives twice, does it only get processed once? Are we listening for all the important events — successful payments, failed payments, subscription changes, cancellations? Is there logging so we can investigate when something goes wrong?
service.whyItMatters: Payment notifications are how your app knows someone paid. If one gets missed, a paying customer loses access. If one gets processed twice, someone gets charged twice.

service.title: Does your app agree with your payment provider about who's paid?
service.prompt: Check that our app and our payment provider (Stripe, Paddle, etc.) always agree on subscription status. If someone upgrades, downgrades, or cancels through the provider's dashboard (not just our UI), does our app update? Is there a way to detect when they get out of sync? Are we using the provider's built-in proration (partial billing) instead of trying to calculate it ourselves?
service.whyItMatters: If your database says someone is paying but Stripe says they canceled, either they're getting free access or you're losing track of revenue.

service.title: Is payment data kept secure?
service.prompt: Check our payment setup for basic security. Are secret API keys only used on the server (never in browser code)? Do credit card numbers show up anywhere in our logs or error tracking? Are we using the payment provider's pre-built payment forms (not collecting card numbers ourselves)? Are test and live modes properly separated?
service.whyItMatters: Handling credit card data incorrectly can result in fines and losing the ability to accept payments entirely. Using the provider's payment forms keeps you safe.

service.title: What happens when a payment fails?
service.prompt: Check what happens when someone's card gets declined. Is there an automatic retry sequence (trying again in a few days, then a week)? Does the user get an email telling them to update their card? Do they lose access immediately or get a grace period? Is there a page where they can easily update their payment method?
service.whyItMatters: Failed cards cause 20-40% of subscription cancellations. The difference between "immediately cancel" and "retry with a friendly email" can be thousands of dollars in saved revenue.

diy.title: Will payment notifications break if something goes wrong?
diy.prompt: Look at how we handle payment notifications (webhooks). If the same notification arrives twice, does it only get processed once? Do we verify each notification is really from Stripe (not someone faking it)? If notifications arrive out of order, does our code handle it? If our server was down when a notification came in, will it get retried? Is there a record of failed notifications so we can investigate?
diy.whyItMatters: Webhooks are how your app knows someone paid. Miss one and a paying customer loses access. Process one twice and someone gets double-charged.

diy.title: Does every billing scenario actually work?
diy.prompt: Walk through every possible billing situation: someone signs up, upgrades, downgrades, cancels, pauses, reactivates, their trial expires, their card fails. For each one: does the charge amount make sense (especially for mid-month changes)? Does their access update immediately? Do they get a notification? Does your database match what Stripe shows?
diy.whyItMatters: Subscriptions have 15+ possible scenarios. Missing even one means either you're losing money or a customer is getting locked out of something they paid for.

diy.title: Is credit card data being handled safely?
diy.prompt: Search our entire codebase for anything that looks like credit card handling. Are card numbers showing up in any logs? Are we using Stripe's payment forms (Elements/Checkout) so card numbers never touch our server? Are API keys only on the server (never in browser code)? Are test and production environments properly separated?
diy.whyItMatters: Mishandling credit card data can result in fines of $5,000-$100,000 per month and permanently losing the ability to accept payments.

diy.title: Are you quietly losing money?
diy.prompt: Look for ways revenue might be leaking. When a card fails, do we retry a few times before canceling? Do free trials actually end when they're supposed to? Do discount codes have expiration dates and usage limits? Are refunds restricted to authorized users? Does the amount charged always match what the customer sees?
diy.whyItMatters: The average subscription business loses 1-5% of revenue to billing bugs. That's money quietly disappearing every month.

smartMove.reasoning: Handling real money means legal requirements, fraud risk, and hundreds of edge cases across currencies, taxes, and payment methods. Nobody should build their own payment processing. The only question is which service to use.

services.name: Stripe
services.description: The industry standard — handles one-time payments, subscriptions, invoicing, tax, and fraud detection
services.freeTier: No monthly fee — 2.9% + 30 cents per transaction

services.name: Paddle
services.description: Handles global tax and compliance for you — you never think about VAT or sales tax
services.freeTier: 5% + 50 cents per transaction (but no tax headaches)

services.name: LemonSqueezy
services.description: Simpler alternative to Paddle, popular with solo founders and indie products

smartMove.tradeoffs: Transaction fees add up as you grow, and you're subject to the provider's rules on disputes and payouts. Stripe gives more control but more responsibility; Paddle/Lemon handle taxes automatically but take a bigger cut.

Credit card numbers don't appear anywhere in your logs or error reports
Your app and your payment provider always agree on who has an active subscription
Tax is handled by your provider or a tax service (you're not calculating it yourself)
Only authorized people can issue refunds
Users get notified when their payment fails (not just silently cut off)
The payment form uses your provider's pre-built UI (you never touch raw card numbers)

diy.item: Payment notifications are verified as actually from Stripe (signature checked)
Each payment request has a unique ID so retries don't create duplicate charges
Mid-month plan changes calculate the correct prorated amount
Failed cards are retried over 2-4 weeks before canceling (not immediately)
No credit card numbers appear in any logs or error tracking
Your database and Stripe always agree on who's subscribed
Tax calculations cover every country/state you sell to
Refunds have proper authorization and an audit trail
If a payment notification fails to process, it's saved for investigation (not lost)
Money amounts are stored as whole numbers (cents, not dollars with decimals) to avoid rounding errors

didYouKnow.stat: Each payment dispute costs an average of $190 in fees, penalties, and lost product — and if more than 1% of your payments get disputed, your payment provider can shut down your account.

dangerZone.headline: A file upload without limits is an invitation to crash your app or drain your bank account
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: File uploads look straightforward — a button, a progress bar, done. But behind that button is a whole minefield: what happens when someone uploads a 5GB file, or 10,000 tiny files, or a file named '../../../etc/passwd', or an image that's actually malware disguised with a fake extension. Then there's making sure files actually get to your storage without corrupting, handling failed uploads that need to resume, and avoiding a surprise $10,000 storage bill because someone uploaded their entire photo library.
dangerZone.failureScenario: You launch your app with a profile picture uploader. Three months in, someone uploads a 200MB file. Your server tries to hold the entire thing in memory while processing it, crashes, and takes down your whole app for 20 minutes. Then your hosting provider sends you a bill for 50GB of bandwidth because that one file has been requested 500 times. Then you discover anyone can directly access any uploaded file by guessing the URL — including files other users thought were private.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: 2-6 months before abuse or a single large file causes a real problem
commonMistakes: No file size limits — someone uploads 2GB and crashes your server
Files saved with their original filename, letting attackers upload malicious paths like '../../system-file'
Uploaded files accessible to anyone who guesses the URL (no access control)
Processing files in memory instead of streaming, causing crashes on large files
No virus scanning — someone uploads malware that infects other users
Image files served at full resolution instead of resized, wasting bandwidth and money

service.title: Are uploads protected from abuse?
service.prompt: Look at our file upload setup. Check these limits: Is there a maximum file size per upload? Is there a limit on total storage per user? Is there a limit on how many files can be uploaded per minute? Are allowed file types restricted (e.g., only images, not executables)? Does the upload service have virus scanning enabled?
service.whyItMatters: Without limits, one person can intentionally or accidentally fill your entire storage quota or crash your server by uploading huge files.

service.title: Can people access files they shouldn't?
service.prompt: Check file access permissions. If someone knows the URL of a file uploaded by another user, can they access it? Are files supposed to be private actually private? When someone deletes a file in your app, does it actually get deleted from storage? Are temporary files cleaned up automatically?
service.whyItMatters: The most common upload mistake is storing everything in a public folder where any URL can be guessed. Private documents become public by accident.

service.title: Will your upload costs spiral out of control?
service.prompt: Check your upload service's pricing and current usage. Are images being automatically resized for different screen sizes (not serving 5MB originals)? Are you paying for bandwidth every time a file is accessed? Is there a cap or alert before costs exceed your budget? Are old/unused files being archived or deleted?
service.whyItMatters: Storage and bandwidth costs can sneak up fast. Serving original 10MB images instead of 200KB thumbnails can multiply your bills by 50x.

service.title: What happens when an upload fails?
service.prompt: Test what happens when uploads fail partway through. Can large uploads resume from where they left off? If someone's connection drops during upload, do they have to start over? Does the UI show clear error messages? Are failed uploads cleaned up so they don't waste storage?
service.whyItMatters: Forcing users to restart a 100MB upload from scratch is how you lose customers. Resumable uploads are critical for anything over a few megabytes.

diy.title: Is your upload code exploitable?
diy.prompt: Look at how we handle uploaded files. Are filenames sanitized before saving (removing characters like ../ or system paths)? Are file extensions checked against actual file content (not just trusting the extension)? Are files scanned for viruses before being made available? Is there validation that uploaded images are actually images (not malware with a .jpg extension)? Are files processed in a way that limits memory usage?
diy.whyItMatters: Trusting user-provided filenames or file types is one of the most common ways apps get compromised. Attackers name files to overwrite system files or sneak in malware.

diy.title: Can anyone access any file?
diy.prompt: Check file access control. If someone knows or guesses a file URL, can they access it without being logged in? Can they access files uploaded by other users? Are files stored with predictable names (like '1.jpg', '2.jpg') that are easy to guess? When checking permissions, does the code verify on the server (not just in the browser)?
diy.whyItMatters: Sequential or guessable file URLs are like numbered doors in a hotel — if you know one, you can try all the others. Private files need random URLs and server-side permission checks.

diy.title: Will large files crash your server?
diy.prompt: Look at how files are processed. Are files loaded entirely into memory before being saved (dangerous for large files)? Is there streaming so files go straight to storage without sitting in memory? Is there a file size limit enforced before processing starts? What happens if disk space runs out?
diy.whyItMatters: Loading a 500MB file into memory to process it can crash your server instantly. Streaming uploads is the difference between handling 10MB files and handling 1GB files.

diy.title: Are you wasting money on storage and bandwidth?
diy.prompt: Check how files are stored and served. Are images automatically resized to multiple sizes (thumbnail, medium, large) or stored only at full resolution? Are files compressed? Are there old or abandoned uploads that never got cleaned up? How much are you paying per GB of storage and bandwidth? Are files served through a CDN or directly from your server?
diy.whyItMatters: Storing one 8MB photo at full resolution instead of generating 50KB, 200KB, and 800KB versions means 10x the bandwidth cost every time it's viewed.

smartMove.reasoning: File uploads seem simple but are full of security and cost traps. Services handle virus scanning, resumable uploads, image optimization, and CDN delivery automatically. The free tiers are generous, and the time saved avoiding storage bugs is worth it.

services.name: UploadThing
services.description: Modern upload service built for Next.js — handles files, images, and videos with built-in optimization
services.freeTier: 2GB storage and 2GB bandwidth per month free

services.name: Uploadcare

services.freeTier: 3,000 uploads and 3GB storage free per month

services.name: Cloudinary
services.description: Powerful image and video processing — resize, crop, optimize, and transform on the fly
services.freeTier: 25GB storage and 25GB bandwidth per month free

smartMove.tradeoffs: You're locked into their pricing as you scale, and switching providers means migrating all your files. But the security, automatic optimization, and virus scanning you get in return are worth it unless you have very specific needs.

There's a storage limit per user to prevent abuse
Images are automatically resized for different uses (not served at full resolution)
Large uploads can resume if the connection drops (not restart from scratch)
Your app has an alert before storage or bandwidth costs exceed your budget
Deleted files are actually removed from storage (not just hidden)
Temporary or abandoned uploads get cleaned up automatically

diy.item: File size is checked before processing starts (upload rejected if too large)
Filenames are sanitized — removing dangerous characters like ../ or system paths
File types are validated by content (not just trusting the extension someone gave it)
Files are streamed to storage (not loaded entirely into memory first)

There's a per-user storage quota to prevent one person from filling the server
Failed or incomplete uploads are cleaned up so they don't waste storage

didYouKnow.stat: The average cost of a data breach involving file uploads is $4.45 million, and 43% of breaches involve uploading malicious files that weren't properly validated.
didYouKnow.source: IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2023

dangerZone.headline: Your perfectly written email is worthless if it lands in spam or never arrives at all
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: Sending email from your app looks simple — you give it a recipient, a subject, a message, hit send. But email providers are paranoid gatekeepers. They check if your domain is properly verified, if you have a history of sending good emails, if your bounce rate is too high, if your content looks spammy. One wrong move and Gmail silently drops everything you send into spam. You'll never even know.
dangerZone.failureScenario: You launch your app. Password reset emails work fine during testing. You get your first 100 signups. Then users start complaining they never got their reset email. You check — nothing in spam, nothing bounced. The emails just vanished. Turns out your sending domain wasn't properly configured, so Gmail quietly blocked you after the first 20 emails. Now 80 people can't log in and you're racing to fix DNS records you didn't know existed.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: Immediate to 2 months — problems show up as soon as you hit real volume
commonMistakes: Sending from a personal Gmail/Outlook account instead of your own domain
Not setting up SPF, DKIM, and DMARC records (the authentication that proves you're legit)
No tracking of bounces and spam complaints, so your sender reputation slowly tanks
Sending password resets and marketing emails from the same address (one gets you marked as spam, ruins both)
Using words like "free", "urgent", or too many exclamation points that trigger spam filters

service.title: Is your domain properly set up for sending?
service.prompt: Check our email sending domain configuration. Are SPF, DKIM, and DMARC records set up correctly? Are we sending from our own domain (like hello@ourapp.com) or a generic one? Is the "from" address an actual mailbox someone checks, or a no-reply that bounces? Show me what our current DNS records look like and if anything's missing.
service.whyItMatters: Email providers use these records to decide if you're legitimate or a spammer. Missing even one can cut your delivery rate in half.

service.title: Are bounces and spam complaints being tracked?
service.prompt: Check if we're monitoring email delivery health. When an email bounces (doesn't exist or inbox full), do we record it and stop sending to that address? Are spam complaints tracked? Do we get notified if our bounce rate spikes? Is there a way to see our current sender reputation score?
service.whyItMatters: Every bounce and spam complaint damages your sender reputation. If it gets bad enough, email providers will block everything from your domain.

service.title: Are different email types properly separated?
service.prompt: Check how we categorize our emails. Are transactional emails (password resets, receipts) sent separately from marketing emails (newsletters, promotions)? Do they use different sending domains or subdomains? Are users able to unsubscribe from marketing without losing critical account emails?
service.whyItMatters: If your marketing emails get marked as spam, it can poison your transactional emails too. They should be completely separate.

service.title: Can you prove an email was sent and received?
service.prompt: Check our email logging and tracking. For critical emails (password resets, payment confirmations), can we see exactly when it was sent, when it was delivered, and if it was opened? If a customer says they never got an email, can we prove we sent it? Is this data kept long enough to investigate disputes?
service.whyItMatters: When a customer says "I never got the email" you need to know if it's a spam filter issue, a typo, or actually your fault.

diy.title: Is your sending setup going to work at scale?
diy.prompt: Look at how we're sending emails. Are we using a proper email service API or trying to send directly from the server? Is there rate limiting so we don't hit sending caps? If the email service is temporarily down, do emails get queued and retried? Are we handling the service's response correctly when an email fails?
diy.whyItMatters: Sending email directly from your server almost guarantees spam folder. And if you hit your service's rate limit, critical emails just disappear.

diy.title: Are critical emails actually getting through?
diy.prompt: Check our most important emails — password resets, signup confirmations, payment receipts. For each one: is the subject line clear and not spammy? Is there a text-only version (not just HTML)? Are images hosted reliably? Are we personalizing with the user's name? Do links actually work? Test sending to Gmail, Outlook, and Apple Mail.
diy.whyItMatters: If password reset emails don't arrive, people can't log in. If payment receipts go to spam, you get support requests and disputes.

diy.title: Are you accidentally looking like a spammer?
diy.prompt: Review our email content for spam triggers. Are we using excessive capitalization or exclamation points? Do subject lines contain words like "free", "urgent", "act now"? Are we sending the same email template to many people at once without personalization? Is there a physical address and unsubscribe link (required by law)? Are we respecting unsubscribe requests immediately?
diy.whyItMatters: Spam filters look for patterns. Even legitimate emails can get blocked if they follow the same patterns as scams.

diy.title: What happens when emails fail?
diy.prompt: Check our email error handling. When an email bounces (address doesn't exist), do we record it and stop trying? When someone marks us as spam, do we remove them from the list? If our email service is down, do we show the user a helpful error? Are failed critical emails (like password resets) retried or just lost?
diy.whyItMatters: Continuing to send to bad addresses ruins your sender reputation. And if a password reset fails silently, the user is just stuck.

smartMove.reasoning: Email deliverability is a full-time job. Services monitor your reputation, handle bounces, warm up your domain, and maintain relationships with inbox providers. The free tiers are generous enough for most apps. Don't try to save $10/month and end up in spam.

services.name: Resend
services.description: Modern API, React email templates, great for developers — newer but gaining fast

services.name: Postmark
services.description: Best-in-class deliverability for transactional emails (signups, resets, receipts)

services.name: SendGrid
services.description: Established player with good free tier — marketing + transactional

smartMove.tradeoffs: Services charge per email after the free tier, and you're trusting them to actually deliver. But trying to handle deliverability yourself means becoming an email infrastructure expert.

dangerZone.headline: Real-time features work great with 5 users — then mysteriously fall apart with 500
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: Making something update live on the screen feels like magic when it works. Behind that magic is a constant connection between every user and your server that needs to survive spotty wifi, browser sleep modes, and server restarts. Each connection uses memory, and suddenly you're managing thousands of open connections that all need to know when something changes. One user's browser going to sleep and waking up can create a cascade of duplicate updates.
dangerZone.failureScenario: You build a real-time dashboard. Works perfectly in testing. You launch and get 200 concurrent users. Your server runs out of memory from holding all those connections open. Users start seeing stale data because their connections silently died and never reconnected. Support tickets pile up saying "the dashboard isn't updating" but when they refresh, it's fine.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: 2-6 months when you hit your first traffic spike
commonMistakes: Connections that die silently when wifi drops or browser sleeps — user thinks they're seeing live data but it's frozen
Every new connection subscribes to every update, so adding users makes everything slower for everyone
No tracking of which connections are actually alive — server holds thousands of dead connections in memory
Updates sent to every single user instead of just the ones who need to see them
Reconnecting creates duplicate subscriptions, so users see the same update 3-4 times

service.title: Will connections survive real-world conditions?
service.prompt: Check how our live update service (Pusher, Ably, etc.) handles connection problems. What happens when someone's wifi drops for 10 seconds? When their laptop goes to sleep and wakes up? When they switch from wifi to mobile data? Do they automatically reconnect? Do they get any updates they missed while disconnected? Is there visual feedback showing connection status?
service.whyItMatters: Real users have spotty connections. If your live updates silently die when wifi hiccups, users think they're seeing current data when they're not.

service.title: Are you only sending updates to people who need them?
service.prompt: Look at how we've set up channels or rooms in our live update service. When something updates, does it only notify users who are looking at that specific thing (not everyone)? Are users unsubscribed from channels when they navigate away? Can someone subscribe to channels they shouldn't have access to?
service.whyItMatters: Sending every update to every user kills performance as you grow. It's like announcing every email to an entire office instead of just the recipient.

service.title: Can your service handle your traffic?
service.prompt: Check our live update service plan and limits. How many concurrent connections does our plan support? How many messages per month? Are we monitoring usage so we know when we're approaching limits? What happens if we exceed limits — does it fail gracefully or just break?
service.whyItMatters: Free tiers on WebSocket services run out fast. 1,000 concurrent users on a free plan means everyone gets disconnected when user 1,001 shows up.

service.title: Is live data secured properly?
service.prompt: Review security for our live update channels. Are channels locked down so users only see data they're authorized to see? Are channel names predictable (like user-123) where someone could guess others? When someone subscribes to a channel, do we verify they should have access? Are sensitive updates encrypted?
service.whyItMatters: Live updates bypass normal page security. If channels aren't locked down, changing a channel name in browser dev tools could let someone watch updates meant for other users.

diy.title: Will connections stay alive and reconnect?
diy.prompt: Look at our WebSocket connection code. What happens when the connection drops — does it automatically try to reconnect? How many times does it retry and with what delays? When it reconnects, does it resubscribe to the right channels without creating duplicates? Is there a visual indicator showing connection status? Does it handle the case where the server restarts?
diy.whyItMatters: WebSocket connections break constantly — wifi drops, servers restart, browsers sleep. If reconnection isn't bulletproof, users get frozen data and don't realize it.

diy.title: Can your server handle many connections?
diy.prompt: Check how our server manages WebSocket connections. Is there a limit on concurrent connections? Does each connection get cleaned up when a user leaves (or do dead connections pile up)? How much memory does each connection use? Is there monitoring to see how many connections are actually open right now?
diy.whyItMatters: Each WebSocket connection holds memory on your server. Without cleanup, dead connections pile up until your server crashes.

diy.title: Are updates being sent efficiently?
diy.prompt: Look at how we broadcast updates. When something changes, are we sending it only to connections that care about that specific data? Or are we sending every update to every connection? When 100 people are watching the same data, does it send one update that fans out, or does it process the same update 100 separate times?
diy.whyItMatters: Inefficient broadcasting creates an exponential slowdown. If each update takes 10ms and you're sending it individually to 1,000 connections, that's 10 seconds to notify everyone.

diy.title: Can someone see updates they shouldn't?
diy.prompt: Review authorization for WebSocket channels. When someone subscribes to a channel, do we verify they have permission to see that data? Can someone guess channel names and subscribe to updates about other users or organizations? Are updates filtered on the server (not just the client)?
diy.whyItMatters: WebSockets often bypass the normal security checks that protect your pages. Someone could subscribe to channels in the browser console and watch data they shouldn't see.

smartMove.reasoning: WebSockets are deceptively hard to scale. Connection management, reconnection logic, and message fan-out are all things that look simple until you have real traffic. Services have solved these problems across millions of connections. Unless you have very specific requirements or are building the next Figma, use a service.

services.name: Pusher
services.description: Dead simple pub/sub for live updates — great docs, works with everything

services.name: Supabase Realtime
services.description: Built into Supabase — if you're already using it, this is the easiest choice
services.freeTier: Included with Supabase free tier (500 concurrent connections)

services.name: Ably
services.description: More features than Pusher, better for complex scenarios like presence and history

services.name: PartyKit
services.description: Edge-first, great for multiplayer and collaborative features, runs on Cloudflare
services.freeTier: Free for development, pay-as-you-go in production

smartMove.tradeoffs: You're paying per connection and per message, which can get expensive at scale. Migration is painful if you ever need to switch. But getting it right yourself requires deep infrastructure knowledge most teams don't have.

Users reconnect automatically when their connection drops
Updates only go to users subscribed to the specific channel (not broadcast to everyone)
There's visual feedback showing whether the live connection is active or dead
Users can't subscribe to channels they shouldn't have access to
You're monitoring connection count and message volume against your plan limits
The app still works (with manual refresh) if the live update service goes down
Reconnecting doesn't create duplicate subscriptions
Channel names aren't easily guessable (not just user-123, order-456)
Users who navigate away from a page unsubscribe from its channels

diy.item: Connections automatically reconnect when they drop (with exponential backoff)
There's a maximum number of connections per server with monitoring
Dead connections get cleaned up within a few minutes
When a server restarts, all clients reconnect successfully
Updates are only sent to connections subscribed to the relevant channel
Channel subscriptions are authorized on the server (not just client-side)
There's a visible connection status indicator for users
Reconnecting doesn't cause duplicate messages or duplicate subscriptions
You can see current connection count and identify memory leaks
Sensitive data in live updates is filtered per user's permissions

didYouKnow.stat: WebSocket connections use 10-50x more memory than regular HTTP requests, and a single server can typically handle 10,000-60,000 concurrent connections before running out of memory.
didYouKnow.source: AWS Architecture Blog on WebSocket scaling patterns

dangerZone.headline: Bad search is invisible until people start looking for things — then it becomes the reason they leave
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: Search feels simple when you're testing with 10 items. Type a word, see matching results. But good search needs to handle typos ("iphone" should find "iPhone"), understand that "laptop" and "computer" are related, rank results so the best ones come first, stay fast when you have 100,000 items, and work across multiple fields at once. The difference between basic matching and actual search is bigger than it looks.
dangerZone.failureScenario: You launch a marketplace with 5,000 products. Search works fine in testing. Six months in, someone searches for "wireles headphons" and gets zero results even though you have 200 wireless headphones. They try "bluetooth earbuds" — still nothing because your product titles say "headphones." Meanwhile your competitor's search just works. You never hear from that person again.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: 3-6 months as your catalog grows and search expectations rise
commonMistakes: Exact matching only — one typo returns zero results instead of guessing what they meant
Searching only titles or names, missing descriptions, tags, or other relevant fields
No ranking logic — a barely-related match shows up before the perfect match
Search gets slower and slower as you add more items until it times out
Searching for "new york" requires the exact phrase in that exact order instead of finding "york, new" or documents with both words

service.title: Does search handle typos and variations?
service.prompt: Test our search with common mistakes and variations. Try misspellings ("wireles headphons"), different word orders ("york new" vs "new york"), singular vs plural ("shoe" vs "shoes"), and related terms ("laptop" when the item says "computer"). Does it still find the right things? Are results ranked sensibly — best matches first?
service.whyItMatters: People don't type perfectly. If one typo breaks your search, you're losing customers who would have bought if they could find what they're looking for.

service.title: Is search actually fast?
service.prompt: Test search speed under realistic conditions. Search for common terms and see how long results take to appear. Try it with your current data size and imagine 10x more items — does your search service have limits on how many records you can search? Are you using the service's features to pre-index content, or searching the raw database every time?
service.whyItMatters: People expect search results instantly — within 100 milliseconds. If it takes even one second, it feels broken.

service.title: Are you searching the right things?
service.prompt: Check what fields are actually being searched. Are we only searching titles/names, or also descriptions, tags, categories, and other relevant content? When someone searches for a product feature or benefit, does it find items that have that in the description? Can filters (like price range or category) be combined with search?
service.whyItMatters: Searching only titles is like only reading the first sentence of every page. You're missing most of the information that would help someone find what they need.

service.title: Will search break as you grow?
service.prompt: Check our search service plan limits. How many records can we search? How many searches per month? What happens if we hit those limits — does search just stop working? Are we close to any limits now? Does our plan include features like typo tolerance and ranking, or do we need to upgrade for those?
service.whyItMatters: Search services have tiers. You might be on a free plan that works great for 1,000 items but silently breaks or gets expensive at 10,000.

diy.title: Does your search handle anything beyond exact matches?
diy.prompt: Test our search implementation with typos, plurals, and variations. Search for "wireles" when the correct spelling is "wireless." Search for "shoe" when items say "shoes." Search for two words in reverse order. What happens? Is there any fuzzy matching, or does it require exact text? Show me exactly what search logic we're using.
diy.whyItMatters: Exact-match search feels broken to users because they're used to Google. One typo shouldn't return zero results.

diy.title: Are results ranked or just random?
diy.prompt: Search for a term that matches multiple items. How are results ordered? Is there any logic putting the best matches first, or is it just random/chronological? If someone's exact search term is in a title, does that appear before items where it's buried in the description? Can users sort results?
diy.whyItMatters: Finding 100 results doesn't help if the perfect match is on page 7. Ranking is the difference between useful and useless.

diy.title: How slow will search get?
diy.prompt: Check how our search actually works under the hood. Is it scanning every record on every search, or using an index? What happens when we have 10x or 100x more data? If we're using database full-text search, is it optimized with proper indexes? How long does a typical search take right now?
diy.whyItMatters: Search that scans every record works fine with 1,000 items. At 100,000 items it times out. By then you're stuck rewriting everything.

diy.title: What happens when search breaks?
diy.prompt: What happens if search is running slow or timing out? Does the whole page crash, or does it show a helpful error? Is there a fallback like "browse all items" or category navigation? Are search errors logged so we can investigate? If search completely fails, can people still use the app?
diy.whyItMatters: Search will eventually have problems. The question is whether your app degrades gracefully or just shows an error screen.

smartMove.reasoning: It depends on what you're searching and how many items. For a directory with 50 entries, database search is fine. For an e-commerce site with 5,000 products, a search service like Algolia pays for itself in customers who can actually find things. The breaking point is around 1,000-5,000 items or when people start expecting instant, typo-tolerant search.

services.name: Algolia
services.description: The gold standard — handles typos, ranking, instant results, and provides ready-made UI components

services.name: Typesense
services.description: Open source alternative you can self-host or use their cloud — similar features to Algolia

services.name: Meilisearch
services.description: Another solid open-source option with good typo tolerance and fast results

smartMove.tradeoffs: Search services cost money at scale (Algolia can get expensive past the free tier). Self-hosting is cheaper but means you manage infrastructure. Database full-text search is free but limited. Start simple, upgrade when you feel the pain.

Search covers all relevant fields (not just titles)
You're within your service's record and search limits
Search has a fallback if the service is temporarily down
Search terms are logged so you can see what people look for

diy.item: Search handles at least one-letter typos without returning zero results
Searching for multiple words finds results with those words in any order
Results are ranked by relevance (exact matches first)
Search uses database indexes (not scanning every record)
Search stays fast with at least 10x your current data size
If search fails or times out, the app shows a helpful message (not a crash)
Search terms and zero-result searches are logged for analysis
There's an alternative way to browse if search isn't working

didYouKnow.stat: 43% of website visitors go straight to the search bar, and those searchers are 2-3x more likely to convert than people who browse — but only if search actually works.

dangerZone.headline: When background tasks fail silently, your app looks like it's working but isn't
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: Running a task in the background seems simple — just fire it off and move on. But what happens when it fails halfway through? How do you know it failed? Does it try again? If a task takes 30 seconds and you get 100 requests at once, do they all run simultaneously and crash your server? Background tasks are invisible until they break, and when they break, the symptoms show up somewhere completely different — missing emails, stale data, incomplete reports.
dangerZone.failureScenario: You built a feature that sends a summary email every night at midnight. It works perfectly for two months. Then your email service has a 10-minute outage at 12:03 AM. Your background task fails once and never runs again — no retry, no notification. Three weeks later a customer asks why they stopped getting emails and you realize hundreds of users have been missing them. You have no logs and no way to tell who missed what.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: 2-6 months before a silent failure becomes a visible problem
commonMistakes: Tasks that fail once and never retry — the app just moves on
No way to tell if a task succeeded or failed unless you manually check logs
Tasks that run forever if something goes wrong (no timeout)
Multiple tasks trying to do the same thing at once because there's no lock
Putting tasks in a list that gets lost if the server restarts

service.title: What happens when a task fails?
service.prompt: Look at how we've set up our background task service (Inngest, Trigger.dev, etc). Check: If a task fails, does it automatically retry? How many times? Is there a delay between retries that gets longer each time? Are we getting notified when tasks fail repeatedly? Can we see a history of which tasks ran and which failed?
service.whyItMatters: Tasks fail all the time — API timeouts, rate limits, temporary outages. The difference between "retry automatically" and "fail once and give up" is the difference between 99% reliability and constant broken features.

service.title: Are tasks properly isolated from each other?
service.prompt: Check how tasks are organized. If one task crashes or runs forever, does it block other tasks from running? Are there timeouts so a stuck task eventually gives up? If we suddenly get 1000 tasks queued up, does the service handle it gracefully or does everything grind to a halt?
service.whyItMatters: One broken task shouldn't bring down your entire background processing system. Without proper isolation, a single stuck task can freeze everything.

service.title: Can you debug what happened?
service.prompt: Look at our task monitoring setup. Can we see which tasks are currently running? Can we look back and see what failed yesterday? Do failed tasks show error messages? Can we manually retry a specific failed task? Is there alerting when tasks start failing at a high rate?
service.whyItMatters: When something breaks at 3 AM, you need to know what broke and why. Without logs and monitoring, you're flying blind.

service.title: Are scheduled tasks actually reliable?
service.prompt: Check our scheduled tasks (cron jobs). If the server restarts or deploys during the scheduled time, does the task still run? If a scheduled task takes longer than the interval (e.g., a 10-minute task that runs every 5 minutes), does it queue up properly or create overlapping runs? Are scheduled tasks in UTC or local time — and does that cause issues with daylight saving time?
service.whyItMatters: Scheduled tasks are easy to set up but hard to get right. Miss one scheduled run and data gets stale or emails don't send.

diy.title: Do failed tasks retry properly?
diy.prompt: Walk through our retry logic. When a task fails, does it try again? How many times? Is there a delay that gets longer between retries (like 1 minute, then 5, then 15)? If it fails all retries, is there a way to investigate what happened? Can the same task get picked up by multiple workers at once, causing it to run twice?
diy.whyItMatters: Without retry logic, temporary failures (like a brief API timeout) become permanent data loss. Tasks need to be resilient.

diy.title: What happens if the server crashes?
diy.prompt: Check what happens to tasks if the server restarts. Are queued tasks stored somewhere permanent (like a database or Redis) or just in memory? If a task was running when the server crashed, does it get retried or lost? Is there a way to recover tasks that were in progress?
diy.whyItMatters: Servers crash, deployments happen. If your task queue lives only in memory, every restart loses work.

diy.title: Can tasks run forever without timing out?
diy.prompt: Look for tasks that could get stuck. Is there a timeout so tasks eventually give up if they run too long? What happens if an external API we're calling never responds? If a task processes a list of items, what happens if the list has a million items? Are there any infinite loops that could trigger?
diy.whyItMatters: A task without a timeout can run forever, eating resources and blocking other work. Eventually it crashes your server.

diy.title: How are you tracking what's happening?
diy.prompt: Check our monitoring and logging for background tasks. Can we see which tasks are currently running? Are errors logged with enough detail to debug them? Is there a way to see task history — what ran when, what failed? Are we getting alerts when tasks fail repeatedly? Can we tell the difference between a one-time failure and a systemic problem?
diy.whyItMatters: Background tasks are invisible by nature. Without proper logging, you won't know they're broken until a customer complains.

smartMove.reasoning: Background task infrastructure is harder than it looks. You need queues, retries, monitoring, scaling, and failure recovery. Services handle all of this and give you a dashboard to see what's happening. Worth it unless you have a very simple use case or need the absolute lowest latency.

services.name: Inngest
services.description: Event-driven background jobs with built-in retries, scheduling, and a great debugging UI
services.freeTier: Up to 100,000 function runs per month free

services.name: Trigger.dev
services.description: Similar to Inngest with excellent Next.js integration and visual workflow builder

services.name: Vercel Cron + Queues
services.description: If you're already on Vercel, built-in scheduled tasks and queues with zero setup
services.freeTier: Included in Vercel's free tier with limits

smartMove.tradeoffs: Services add another dependency and usually charge by task volume. Self-hosted options like BullMQ give more control but require managing Redis, deployment, and monitoring yourself.

didYouKnow.stat: 47% of background job failures are never detected by the application — they fail silently and only surface when users report missing functionality weeks later.

dangerZone.headline: The difference between helpful updates and getting banned for spam is thinner than you think
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: Sending a notification looks like one line of code. But actually doing it right means tracking what you already sent (so you don't spam), respecting user preferences across multiple channels, handling delivery failures gracefully, not waking people up at 3am, batching updates so one busy thread doesn't send 47 emails, and keeping track of what was delivered so you can debug "I never got that email" complaints. Miss any of this and you're either annoying users or missing critical alerts.
dangerZone.failureScenario: Your app sends email notifications when someone comments. Works great at launch. Three months later, a popular thread gets 200 comments in an hour. Your code sends 200 individual emails to everyone watching that thread. Gmail flags you as spam. Now none of your emails — including password resets — reach anyone. Your domain reputation is tanked and takes months to recover.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: 2-6 months before notification volume causes spam flags or user complaints
commonMistakes: Sending the same notification twice because the code doesn't check what was already sent
No way for users to control what notifications they get — it's all or nothing
Sending emails immediately for every event instead of batching updates from the same source
No rate limiting — one active user or thread can trigger hundreds of notifications
Notifications going out at random times in user timezones (3am wake-up emails)
No tracking of what was delivered, so "I never got it" complaints are impossible to investigate

service.title: Are you respecting user notification preferences?
service.prompt: Check how notification preferences work. Can users control what types of notifications they get? Are these preferences respected across all channels (email, push, SMS)? If someone unsubscribes from emails, do they actually stop getting them? Is there a record of what preferences each user has set? Are legal requirements (like unsubscribe links in emails) being followed?
service.whyItMatters: Ignoring user preferences is how you get spam complaints. Too many complaints and email providers will block everything from your domain — including critical emails like password resets.

service.title: Can the same event spam someone with notifications?
service.prompt: Look at how we handle rapid-fire events. If 50 comments happen on one thread in an hour, does someone subscribed to that thread get 50 separate notifications? Is there batching (grouping updates together)? Is there a limit on how many notifications one user can get in a short time? What happens if someone mentions the same person 10 times in a row?
service.whyItMatters: One busy user or thread shouldn't be able to flood everyone with notifications. Batching "50 new comments" into one notification is better UX and keeps you out of spam folders.

service.title: Are notification failures handled gracefully?
service.prompt: Check what happens when notifications fail. If an email bounces or a push notification can't be delivered, is it recorded? Does the system retry? If a notification service goes down temporarily, do queued notifications get lost or delivered later? Is there a way to see which notifications failed and why?
service.whyItMatters: Email addresses change, phones get new numbers, push tokens expire. If failed notifications aren't tracked, users miss important updates and you can't debug why.

service.title: Are notifications timed reasonably?
service.prompt: Look at when notifications go out. Are time zones considered so users don't get emails at 3am? Are notifications batched so someone doesn't get woken up by multiple pings in the middle of the night? Can users set quiet hours? Are urgent notifications (like security alerts) distinguished from casual ones?
service.whyItMatters: A 3am notification about a non-urgent comment is how you get uninstalls and bad reviews. Respecting time and urgency keeps users happy.

diy.title: Will your notification system collapse under load?
diy.prompt: Check how notifications are sent. Are they queued in the background or sent immediately during the user's request? If 1000 notifications need to go out at once (like a popular post), what happens? Is there rate limiting so we don't hit email/SMS provider limits? Can failed notifications be retried without sending duplicates? Is there logging so we can debug delivery issues?
diy.whyItMatters: Sending notifications during a web request makes your app slow. Sending 1000 at once can crash your email provider or get you flagged for abuse.

diy.title: Are you accidentally spamming people?
diy.prompt: Look for scenarios that could cause notification spam. If someone gets 10 mentions in 10 minutes, do they get 10 separate notifications? If a thread has 50 comments, does everyone subscribed get 50 emails? Is there deduplication so the same event doesn't trigger multiple notifications? Are notifications batched (combined into digests) when appropriate?
diy.whyItMatters: Each spammy notification increases the chance someone unsubscribes or marks you as spam. Too many spam reports and your entire domain gets blacklisted.

diy.title: Can users actually control what they receive?
diy.prompt: Check notification preferences. Can users choose which types of notifications they want? Are these preferences stored and actually checked before sending? Is there a preference UI that's easy to find? Do legal requirements (unsubscribe links in emails, opt-out language) exist? What happens if someone clicks unsubscribe — does it really work?
diy.whyItMatters: Sending notifications people opted out of violates anti-spam laws (CAN-SPAM, GDPR). Fines start at $43,792 per email in the US.

diy.title: Can you prove a notification was sent?
diy.prompt: Look at notification tracking. Is there a record of what was sent, when, and to whom? Can you look up whether a specific user received a specific notification? Are delivery statuses tracked (sent, delivered, bounced, opened)? If someone says "I never got that email", can you investigate?
diy.whyItMatters: You can't debug "I never got my password reset email" complaints without delivery tracking. Also critical for compliance when you need to prove someone was notified.

smartMove.reasoning: Notifications are tricky. For simple use cases (welcome emails, password resets), you can DIY with an email service. But if you need multi-channel (push + email + SMS), user preferences, batching, and delivery tracking, a service saves months of work. The breaking point is usually when you need push notifications or sophisticated preference management.

services.name: Knock
services.description: Handles email, push, SMS, and in-app notifications with user preferences and delivery tracking built in

services.name: Novu
services.description: Open source notification infrastructure — host it yourself or use their cloud, supports all channels

services.name: OneSignal
services.description: Focused on push notifications for web and mobile — great if that's your main channel
services.freeTier: Unlimited push notifications on free tier

services.name: Customer.io
services.description: Better for lifecycle/marketing emails than transactional, includes segmentation and campaigns

smartMove.tradeoffs: Services add another dependency and cost scales with volume. DIY gives full control but you're building preference management, delivery tracking, and batching yourself. If notifications are core to your product, a service is worth it. If it's just occasional emails, DIY is fine.

didYouKnow.stat: Email providers like Gmail and Outlook will block your entire domain if your spam complaint rate exceeds 0.3% — that's just 3 complaints per 1,000 emails sent.

dangerZone.headline: Bad analytics don't just waste time — they convince you to make the wrong decisions with confidence
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: Adding analytics looks simple — drop in a tracking code, watch numbers go up. But meaningful analytics means knowing which events matter, tracking them consistently, filtering out bots and test traffic, respecting privacy laws across different countries, and making sure the numbers you see actually reflect what users are doing. Bad tracking is worse than no tracking because you'll make confident decisions based on garbage data.
dangerZone.failureScenario: You launch and start tracking everything — page views, clicks, sign-ups. Three months in, you notice conversions are dropping. You redesign the whole onboarding flow based on your analytics. Conversions drop further. Turns out 40% of your "users" were actually bots, your "sign-up" event fired twice for real users, and your most engaged customers were filtered out because they use privacy tools that block your analytics.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: Immediately — but you won't notice until you make a bad decision based on bad data
commonMistakes: Tracking events inconsistently (sometimes fires, sometimes doesn't) so the data is unreliable
No way to filter out your own testing, bots, or spam traffic from real users
Tracking personally identifying information (names, emails) without proper consent or encryption
Events named so vaguely ("button_click") that you can't tell what they mean six months later
Cookie banners that don't actually stop tracking when someone says no

service.title: Are you tracking bot and test traffic as real users?
service.prompt: Look at our analytics data and setup. Is there a way to filter out our own team's testing? Are there obvious signs of bot traffic (same exact patterns, inhuman timing)? Do we have a test environment that sends data to a separate analytics project? Can we exclude traffic from specific IP addresses or development domains?
service.whyItMatters: If 30% of your traffic is bots and dev testing, every decision you make is based on data that's 30% wrong. You might optimize for robots.

service.title: Does your tracking respect privacy laws?
service.prompt: Check our analytics against privacy requirements. Are we asking for consent before tracking in Europe (GDPR)? Does our cookie banner actually stop analytics when someone says no, or just hide and keep tracking? Are we collecting any personally identifying info (emails, names, IP addresses)? Do we have a privacy policy that mentions our analytics provider?
service.whyItMatters: GDPR fines start at €20 million or 4% of revenue. Even small sites need consent for most analytics tools, and ignoring it isn't just illegal — it's alienating privacy-conscious users.

service.title: Will you understand your data six months from now?
service.prompt: Review how we've named and organized our analytics events. Are event names descriptive ("checkout_completed") or vague ("click", "action")? Do similar events follow a consistent naming pattern? Is there documentation explaining what each event means and when it fires? Are we tracking the context (which page, which plan, which feature)?
service.whyItMatters: Three months from now when you're trying to understand why conversions dropped, cryptic event names like "btn_1" or "action_complete" will be useless. You'll be guessing what you were tracking.

service.title: Are critical events actually being tracked reliably?
service.prompt: Test our most important analytics events. Do they fire every time they should (not just sometimes)? Do they fire exactly once (not twice or three times on a single action)? If someone has an ad blocker or strict privacy settings, what still gets tracked? Are events sent even if someone closes the page immediately?
service.whyItMatters: If your "purchase completed" event only fires 80% of the time, you'll think sales are dropping when they're not. Or you'll count the same sale twice and think business is better than it is.

diy.title: Are you accidentally breaking privacy laws?
diy.prompt: Search our codebase for what data we're collecting and where it goes. Are we storing or sending IP addresses, emails, or names without consent? Do we track people before they accept cookies? Is tracking data stored in a way that could identify individuals? Do we have any tracking on pages where people enter sensitive info (medical, financial)?
diy.whyItMatters: GDPR violations start at €20 million. Even unintentional tracking of personal data without consent can trigger investigations and fines.

diy.title: Is your tracking implementation actually reliable?
diy.prompt: Walk through every important user action and check that analytics fires correctly. Does it work if someone clicks fast (not just normal speed)? Does it fire before a redirect or page change? Does it work with ad blockers (for non-privacy-violating tracking)? Does it handle errors gracefully (not crash the whole page if analytics fails)?
diy.whyItMatters: Unreliable tracking means you're making decisions based on partial data. If the "checkout" button only tracks 70% of clicks, you'll think checkout is failing when it's your tracking that's broken.

diy.title: Can you separate real users from noise?
diy.prompt: Look at our analytics data for signs of non-human traffic. Are there sessions that are impossibly fast or follow inhuman patterns? Do we track our own team's testing and development work? Are there obvious bot signatures (same user agent, perfect timing)? Is there a way to flag and filter these out?
diy.whyItMatters: If you can't tell bots from humans, you might optimize your site for robots. Real users behave differently than scrapers and automated testing.

diy.title: Will this tracking data actually help you make decisions?
diy.prompt: Review what we're tracking and ask: for each event, what decision would it help us make? Are we tracking too much (drowning in data) or too little (missing important context)? When someone completes an action, do we know which feature they came from? Can we see the full user journey or just isolated events?
diy.whyItMatters: Analytics that can't answer real questions ("Why did users stop here?" "Which feature drives retention?") just creates busywork. You want data that tells you what to build or fix next.

smartMove.reasoning: Basic analytics is easy to implement yourself, but the value comes from analysis tools, privacy compliance, reliable tracking infrastructure, and filtering out noise. Services handle the hard parts and let you focus on using the data instead of babysitting it.

dangerZone.headline: The moment someone requests a data export is when you find out your database can't handle it
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: Export sounds simple — click a button, download a file. But what happens when someone has 50,000 records? Your server tries to load all of them into memory at once, crashes, and takes down your entire app for everyone. Then there's format handling (Excel expects dates differently than JSON), making sure people can only export their own data, and dealing with exports that take minutes to generate.
dangerZone.failureScenario: You're six months in with 1,000 users. A power user who's been with you since day one clicks "Export My Data" to back up their work. Your server tries to load their 80,000 records, runs out of memory, and crashes. Your app goes down for 20 minutes while it restarts. Three other users were in the middle of important work. One of them tweets about it.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: 3-9 months, once someone accumulates enough data
commonMistakes: Loading all export data into memory at once instead of streaming it in chunks
No timeout or size limit — a large export can run forever and crash the server
Export buttons that trigger synchronously, making the user wait 2 minutes staring at a loading spinner
Not checking if the person requesting the export actually owns that data
Generating the same export file over and over instead of caching it for a few minutes
Dates and numbers formatted for one region but exported to users in another

service.title: Can large exports crash your app?
service.prompt: Check how our export feature handles large amounts of data. When someone clicks export, does it process everything at once or in chunks? Is there a limit on export size? Does the export run in the background (so the user can keep using the app) or does it freeze their browser? If we're using a background job service like Inngest or Trigger.dev, are jobs configured with timeouts and memory limits?
service.whyItMatters: A single large export can bring down your entire app for everyone. Background processing keeps your app responsive and limits the blast radius.

service.title: Can people export data that isn't theirs?
service.prompt: Test the export feature with different user accounts. Can you export another user's data by changing an ID in the URL or request? Are multi-user workspaces handled correctly — can team members export team data but not other teams' data? Is there logging of who exported what and when?
service.whyItMatters: Export is one of the easiest ways to accidentally leak data. Someone figures out they can change a number in the URL and suddenly they're downloading everyone's information.

service.title: Do exported files actually work?
service.prompt: Download exports in each format we offer (CSV, JSON, Excel, etc.) and try opening them in the relevant programs. Do dates show up correctly? Do numbers lose precision or get formatted weirdly? Do column headers make sense? Are special characters (like commas in CSV files or quotes) handled correctly? Can the exported data be re-imported if needed?
service.whyItMatters: An export file that can't be opened or has scrambled data is worse than no export at all. You'll get support emails and lose trust.

service.title: What happens when exports take a long time?
service.prompt: Test what happens when an export takes more than 30 seconds to generate. Does the user get any feedback? Can they navigate away and get notified when it's ready? If they click export twice, does it create two jobs or recognize it's already running? Is there a way to cancel a running export?
service.whyItMatters: Large exports can take minutes. If users don't know what's happening, they'll click the button five more times and make things worse.

diy.title: Will a large export bring down your entire app?
diy.prompt: Look at how our export code loads and processes data. Does it try to load everything into memory at once? Is there a limit on how many records can be exported? Does it process data in batches/chunks or all at once? If an export takes more than a few seconds, does it run in the background or freeze the user's browser? What happens if two users request large exports at the same time?
diy.whyItMatters: Loading 100,000 database rows into memory will crash your server and take down your app for everyone. Processing in chunks keeps your app alive.

diy.title: Can someone export data they don't own?
diy.prompt: Check every export endpoint. Does it verify the requesting user actually owns or has permission to access the data being exported? Can you change a user ID, workspace ID, or date range in the request to get someone else's data? Is there an audit log of who exported what? Are there rate limits to prevent someone from exporting your entire database?
diy.whyItMatters: Export endpoints are a common security hole. If someone can change one number and download everyone's data, you have a catastrophic breach on your hands.

diy.title: Do the exported files work correctly?
diy.prompt: Generate test exports with edge cases: dates, large numbers, special characters (commas, quotes, emojis), null values, very long text. Try opening the files in Excel, Google Sheets, text editors. Do dates show up correctly or turn into weird numbers? Do commas in text break CSV columns? Does JSON parse correctly? Are column names clear and consistent?
diy.whyItMatters: Export bugs only surface when someone tries to use the file. If dates are corrupted or the CSV format is broken, you'll get angry support emails and look unprofessional.

diy.title: What happens when exports are slow?
diy.prompt: Check what happens during long exports. Does the user get progress feedback? Can they close the page and come back? Is there a timeout (exports don't run forever)? What happens if the server restarts mid-export? If someone clicks export multiple times, does each click start a new job? How much server memory does a large export consume?
diy.whyItMatters: Exports that take minutes without feedback make users think the app broke. Exports without timeouts can silently consume all your server resources.

smartMove.reasoning: For small amounts of data (under 10,000 records), building export is straightforward. But if exports can get large or take more than a few seconds, you need background processing. At that point, a background job service (Inngest, Trigger.dev) handles the hard parts — queuing, retries, timeouts — for less than building it yourself.

services.name: Inngest
services.description: Background job runner — queue export jobs, handle large datasets in chunks, notify users when done
services.freeTier: Up to 1,000 jobs per month free

services.name: Trigger.dev
services.description: Similar to Inngest but with better real-time progress tracking for long exports
services.freeTier: Free tier includes basic background jobs

services.name: Retool
services.description: Build internal admin tools including custom exports — useful if you need other admin features anyway

smartMove.tradeoffs: Background services add complexity and a monthly bill at scale, but they keep large exports from crashing your app. For small apps with small exports, a simple synchronous download is fine.
