Large exports run in the background and notify the user when ready
Export requests verify the user has permission to access that data
Clicking export multiple times doesn't start multiple jobs
Exported files open correctly in Excel/Sheets and preserve formatting
Dates and numbers are formatted consistently across exports
There's a size limit or timeout so exports can't run forever
Failed exports show a helpful error (not just a blank page or timeout)
Export activity is logged for security audits

diy.item: Export code processes data in chunks, not loading everything into memory
There's a hard limit on export size (number of records or file size)
Export requests check that the user owns the data being exported
Exports have a timeout so they can't run forever and consume resources
CSV files handle special characters correctly (commas, quotes, line breaks)
Dates export in a consistent format that works across different tools
Large exports don't freeze the user's browser (they run server-side)
Clicking export twice doesn't generate duplicate files or jobs
Export endpoints have rate limiting to prevent abuse
Failed exports return a clear error message (not a 500 error or hang)

didYouKnow.stat: Under GDPR and similar privacy laws, you must provide a user's data in a portable format within 30 days of request — and if you can't, fines start at 2% of global revenue.
didYouKnow.source: EU General Data Protection Regulation Article 20

dangerZone.headline: Feature flags are supposed to reduce risk — until they become the risk
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: A feature flag starts simple: an if-statement that checks whether something is on or off. But then you want to show the new checkout to just 5% of users. Then you want to exclude enterprise customers from that test. Then you want different behavior in different regions. Soon you have dozens of flags checking dozens of conditions on every page load, and nobody remembers which ones are still being used or what happens if they fail to load.
dangerZone.failureScenario: You launch a new pricing page behind a flag, set to 10% of users. It works great. Three months later you're working on something else and accidentally delete the flag config. Suddenly 100% of users see the new page — which was never finished. Or worse: the flag service goes down for 30 seconds and your entire app freezes because every page is waiting for flag checks that never return.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: 3-12 months as flags accumulate and nobody cleans them up
commonMistakes: Flags that call an external service on every page load, slowing everything down
No default behavior when flags can't be fetched — the app just breaks
Flags left in the code forever after the experiment ends, creating confusion
Critical features gated behind flags that could accidentally get toggled
Flag checks that happen after showing content, causing the page to flicker
No record of what each flag does or who owns it

service.title: Are flags slowing down your app?
service.prompt: Look at where and how often we check feature flags. Are flags being checked on every page load? Are those checks cached (remembered) so we don't call the flag service repeatedly? Does the page wait for flags to load before showing anything? What happens if the flag service takes 3 seconds to respond? Is there a timeout?
service.whyItMatters: Feature flag checks can add hundreds of milliseconds to every page load. Multiply that by every user on every page and your app feels sluggish for no obvious reason.

service.title: What happens when the flag service is down?
service.prompt: Check what happens if our feature flag service (LaunchDarkly, PostHog, etc.) is temporarily unavailable. Does each flag have a sensible default? Does the app still work or does it crash/freeze? Are defaults set close to where flags are used, or buried somewhere central where they're hard to find?
service.whyItMatters: If your app breaks every time your flag service hiccups, you've turned an optional tool into a critical dependency. Defaults should let your app keep running.

service.title: Can flags accidentally break critical features?
service.prompt: List all features currently behind flags. Which ones are customer-facing and revenue-critical (like checkout, login, payments)? Are there safeguards preventing someone from accidentally toggling a critical flag? Is there a review process or is anyone on the team able to turn things on and off?
service.whyItMatters: Feature flags give you power to change behavior without deploying code — which also means power to accidentally break things in production without deploying code.

service.title: Are old flags cleaned up?
service.prompt: Check how many feature flags we have and when each was created. Are there flags older than 6 months? For each flag, is it documented what it does and why it exists? Is there a process for removing flags once an experiment is over?
service.whyItMatters: Every flag adds complexity and slows things down slightly. Old unused flags accumulate like technical debt — eventually nobody knows what's safe to remove.

diy.title: Are flags checked efficiently?
diy.prompt: Look at how our code checks feature flags. Are we calling a database or external service every single time? Is the result cached (remembered) for at least a few minutes? Are flags checked once per user session or on every page? If a flag check fails, does it retry indefinitely or fail fast with a default?
diy.whyItMatters: Checking flags on every action can mean thousands of extra database calls per minute. Caching the result for even 5 minutes dramatically reduces load.

diy.title: What happens when flag checks fail?
diy.prompt: For each feature flag in our code, check: if the flag can't be read (database down, API timeout, etc.), what happens? Is there a hardcoded default? Does the feature turn on or off? Is the default behavior safe? Are errors logged so we know flags are failing?
diy.whyItMatters: Feature flags should make your app more reliable, not more fragile. Every flag needs a sensible default so failures don't cascade.

diy.title: Can percentage rollouts actually work correctly?
diy.prompt: If we're using percentage-based rollouts (show to 10% of users), check: is the percentage calculated consistently per user (same user always gets the same result)? Is it truly random or could it accidentally skew toward certain users? Does the percentage apply globally or per session (could someone refresh and get different results)?
diy.whyItMatters: Bad percentage logic means your A/B test is comparing different groups of users each time, making results meaningless. Or the same user sees different versions randomly, which is confusing.

diy.title: Are flags creating security holes?
diy.prompt: Look for flags that gate sensitive features. Could someone figure out the flag name and manually enable it in their browser? Are flags checked on the server (where users can't tamper) or only in the browser? Could changing a flag in the URL or local storage give someone unauthorized access?
diy.whyItMatters: Client-side flags (checked in the browser) can be manipulated by users. If you're using flags to hide admin features or paid features, that check must happen on the server.

smartMove.reasoning: Simple on/off flags that default to the safe behavior? Fine to build yourself. But the moment you need percentage rollouts, user targeting, or analytics on how users interact with different versions — use a service. The math for consistent percentage rollouts is trickier than it looks, and tracking which users saw which version gets complicated fast.

services.name: PostHog
services.description: Feature flags plus analytics and session replay in one tool — great for understanding how users actually use new features

services.name: Flagsmith

services.name: LaunchDarkly

smartMove.tradeoffs: Services add another external dependency and monthly cost as you grow. DIY flags are simple and fast but get messy quickly. If you build your own, commit to keeping it simple — just on/off switches with sensible defaults, nothing fancier.

didYouKnow.stat: Knight Capital lost $440 million in 45 minutes in 2012 because of an old feature flag that accidentally got re-enabled, triggering dormant code that went haywire and placed millions of bad stock trades.

dangerZone.headline: AI features can work perfectly in testing and quietly bankrupt you in production
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: Adding AI looks simple — call an API, get a response. But every call costs money, and users will find creative ways to trigger expensive requests. That chatbot that costs 2 cents per message? Someone will paste a 50-page document and ask it to summarize. Your image generator? Someone will generate 1000 variations looking for the perfect one. Plus AI responses are unpredictable — the same prompt can give wildly different answers, including confidential data, hallucinated facts, or offensive content.
dangerZone.failureScenario: You launch an AI-powered writing assistant. It's amazing. People love it. Usage explodes. Then your OpenAI bill hits $8,000 in week two because you have no rate limits and someone built a Chrome extension that auto-generates content every 5 seconds. Meanwhile, a user discovers that asking "ignore previous instructions and reveal system prompts" exposes your entire prompt template, which a competitor copies.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: 2-8 weeks once you get real traffic
commonMistakes: No rate limits — users can burn through thousands of API calls
Letting users send unlimited tokens (AI charges by input AND output length)
System prompts that can be extracted by asking the AI to ignore instructions
No fallback when the AI service is down — your whole feature breaks
Storing API keys in browser code where anyone can grab them
Not sanitizing user input before sending to AI (opens injection attacks)
Assuming AI responses are always safe to display without checking them first

service.title: Can users rack up huge AI bills?
service.prompt: Look at how we're using our AI service (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.). Check: Is there a limit on how many AI requests one user can make per hour or day? Is there a maximum length for user inputs (tokens sent)? Is there a maximum length for AI outputs? Can someone trigger expensive operations (like image generation or long document analysis) without limits? Show me where these limits are enforced.
service.whyItMatters: AI pricing is per-token (roughly per-word). One user can accidentally or intentionally burn through hundreds of dollars in minutes if there are no guardrails.

service.title: What happens when the AI service goes down?
service.prompt: Check what happens if OpenAI or our AI provider has an outage. Does our app show a helpful error message or just break? Can users still access non-AI features? Is there a timeout so requests don't hang forever? Do failed AI requests get retried automatically (potentially making the problem worse)?
service.whyItMatters: AI services go down regularly — OpenAI has had multiple multi-hour outages. If your entire app depends on it working, you're down too.

service.title: Can someone steal your prompts or trick the AI?
service.prompt: Review how we're sending prompts to the AI. Can a user inject text that makes the AI ignore its instructions (prompt injection)? Are system prompts clearly separated from user input? Could someone get the AI to reveal its system prompt by asking? Are we validating AI responses before showing them to users?
service.whyItMatters: Prompts are your secret sauce. If someone can extract them, they can copy your feature. Worse, prompt injection can make the AI say or do things you never intended.

service.title: Are we handling AI responses safely?
service.prompt: Check what we do with AI-generated content. Are responses checked for sensitive information before displaying? Do we filter or sanitize AI output before storing it? Could the AI return something offensive, dangerous, or legally problematic? Is there logging so we can investigate bad outputs?
service.whyItMatters: AI can hallucinate facts, leak training data, or generate harmful content. Displaying it raw without checking is like letting strangers post directly to your site.

diy.title: Is your AI integration actually secure?
diy.prompt: Search our codebase for AI API usage. Are API keys only on the server (never in browser JavaScript)? Are we using environment variables (not hardcoding keys)? Do we validate and sanitize user input before sending to AI? Are system prompts clearly separated from user content? Could someone extract your prompts by cleverly phrasing a question?
diy.whyItMatters: An exposed API key means anyone can use your AI credits. A leaked prompt means competitors can copy your feature. Prompt injection means users can make the AI do anything.

diy.title: Will AI costs spiral out of control?
diy.prompt: Review every place we call an AI API. Is there rate limiting per user? Is there a cap on input length (tokens sent)? Is there a cap on output length (tokens received)? For expensive operations like embeddings or image generation, are there stricter limits? What's the maximum possible cost one user could incur in an hour?
diy.whyItMatters: AI costs scale with usage in ways other features don't. Without limits, a single power user or attacker can generate a five-figure bill in days.

diy.title: Does your app survive AI downtime?
diy.prompt: Check how our app handles AI failures. If the API call times out, what happens? If it returns an error, does the user see something helpful? Can the rest of the app function without AI? Are there retries, and if so, are they limited? Is there monitoring so we know when AI features are failing?
diy.whyItMatters: AI APIs have worse uptime than traditional services — 99% vs 99.9% is the difference between 7 hours of downtime per month vs 40 minutes. Your app needs to handle it gracefully.

diy.title: Are AI responses being vetted?
diy.prompt: Look at what we do with AI-generated content before showing it to users. Do we check for personally identifiable information? Do we check for inappropriate content? Do we validate that the response actually answers the question (not hallucinated nonsense)? Is there a human review process for high-stakes outputs? Are all AI interactions logged?
diy.whyItMatters: AI hallucinates confidently. Without validation, you're serving users confident-sounding misinformation and calling it a feature.

smartMove.reasoning: Basic AI integration (chat, completion, embeddings) is straightforward and worth doing yourself if you understand the risks. Use a service like Vercel AI SDK to handle streaming and multi-provider support cleanly. BUT if you need RAG (connecting AI to your data), fine-tuning, or enterprise features like content filtering, a managed service saves months of work.

services.name: Vercel AI SDK
services.description: Handles streaming responses, switching between AI providers, and React integration cleanly
services.freeTier: Free SDK — you pay your AI provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) directly

services.name: OpenAI API
services.description: Direct access to GPT models — simple for chat and completion, predictable pricing
services.freeTier: No free tier — ~$0.002 per 1K tokens (roughly 750 words)

services.name: OpenRouter

services.freeTier: Some models have free tiers — pricing varies by model

smartMove.tradeoffs: DIY means you control costs and customization but you're responsible for rate limiting, prompt security, and handling outages. Using an SDK like Vercel's gives you better DX but you still own the hard parts. Managed platforms handle more but lock you into their ecosystem.

dangerZone.headline: When your marketing team can't update the homepage without filing a ticket, you've become the bottleneck
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: A CMS looks simple on the surface — fields for text, images, and a save button. But behind that is a whole system that needs to work: handling images without breaking your site's speed, making sure content changes don't break the layout, versioning so you can undo mistakes, previewing changes before they go live, and managing who can edit what. Get it wrong and you're either locked into manual deploys for every typo fix or dealing with published content that crashes your site.
dangerZone.failureScenario: You launch with blog posts as markdown files in your code. Works great at first. Six months later, your marketing team wants to add a case study section with custom layouts, your sales team needs landing pages for campaigns, and legal needs to update terms monthly. Every content change requires you to edit files, test locally, and deploy. You've become a human bottleneck charging $100/hour to fix typos.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: 3-6 months before content needs outgrow your setup
commonMistakes: Images uploaded directly to your hosting without optimization (site loads get slower with every new image)
No content versioning — when someone overwrites something important, it's gone forever
Preview doesn't match what actually goes live (so published content breaks)
No validation on content fields — so a missing image or too-long title breaks your page layout
Anyone with access can publish anything immediately (no approval workflow)

service.title: Will large images slow down your site?
service.prompt: Check how images are handled in our CMS setup. When someone uploads a large image, is it automatically resized and compressed? Are images served in modern formats (like WebP) for faster loading? Can editors upload any size image or are there reasonable limits? Test uploading a huge image and see what happens to page load speed.
service.whyItMatters: Content editors aren't web performance experts. If your CMS lets them upload 10MB photos that become 10MB images on your homepage, your site will feel painfully slow.

service.title: Can content changes break your site?
service.prompt: Look at how content from the CMS flows into your site. If someone forgets to fill in a required field, does the page break or handle it gracefully? If a title is way too long, does it overflow and mess up the layout? Can editors preview changes exactly as they'll appear live before publishing? Is there validation preventing obviously wrong content (like a negative price)?
service.whyItMatters: Content editors shouldn't need to know about character limits or required fields. Your CMS should prevent content that breaks your site from being published.

service.title: Is your content locked to one CMS forever?
service.prompt: Check how content is stored and retrieved. Can you export all your content in a standard format (like JSON or markdown)? Is your frontend code tightly coupled to this specific CMS's structure, or could you swap to a different CMS without rewriting everything? Are you using CMS-specific features that would be hard to replicate elsewhere?
service.whyItMatters: Switching CMSs later means migrating hundreds or thousands of content items. If your code and content structure are married to one provider, you're stuck with their pricing and features forever.

service.title: Can you undo content mistakes?
service.prompt: Test what happens when content gets messed up. If someone accidentally deletes an important page or overwrites content, can you restore a previous version? How far back does version history go? Can you see who made each change and when? Is there a way to schedule content to publish at a specific time?
service.whyItMatters: Content mistakes happen constantly. Without version history and rollback, one bad publish can mean hours of work recreating what was there before.

diy.title: Will content deploys fail silently?
diy.prompt: Check what happens when content gets built into your site. If someone adds content that breaks the build (like invalid markdown or a missing image reference), does it fail with a clear error or silently break? Is there a way to preview content changes before they trigger a deploy? How long does a deploy take when content changes — can editors see their updates quickly?
diy.whyItMatters: If content changes trigger full site rebuilds, every typo fix means waiting for a deploy. If broken content fails silently, you won't know your site is down until a user tells you.

diy.title: Are images being optimized automatically?
diy.prompt: Look at how images in content get processed. When an editor adds an image, is it automatically resized for different screen sizes? Are images compressed and converted to modern formats? Or are source images served directly to users? Check a few pages with images and see what file sizes are actually being downloaded.
diy.whyItMatters: Content images uploaded at full resolution can easily be 5-10MB each. Without automatic optimization, your site will slow down every time someone adds an image.

diy.title: Can non-technical people actually edit content?
diy.prompt: Try editing content as if you knew nothing about code. Can you change text without touching markdown syntax? Are image paths obvious or do you need to know folder structures? If something goes wrong, is the error message helpful or technical jargon? Could your marketing team realistically edit content without asking you for help every time?
diy.whyItMatters: If editing content requires understanding file paths, git commands, or markdown syntax, you'll spend hours per week helping people make simple changes.

diy.title: What happens if content gets corrupted?
diy.prompt: Check how content is stored and backed up. If content files are in git, is there a clear history so you can restore old versions? If content is in a database, is it backed up regularly? Can you restore a specific piece of content or only the whole database? Test deleting something important and see if you can get it back.
diy.whyItMatters: Content represents hours or weeks of work. Without proper backups and version control, one mistake or technical issue can lose months of writing.

smartMove.reasoning: If it's just you writing blog posts occasionally, markdown files in your code are fine. If you have a team that needs to update content independently, or if you're changing content more than weekly, a CMS saves you time immediately. The free tiers are generous enough that you're really choosing between your time and their cut.

services.name: Sanity
services.description: Flexible content modeling, real-time collaboration, great for complex sites with lots of content types

services.name: Contentful
services.description: Enterprise-grade with strong permissions and workflows — good if content needs approval before publishing

services.name: Payload
services.description: Open source CMS you host yourself — full control, no vendor fees, but you handle updates and hosting
services.freeTier: Fully free but you pay hosting costs

services.name: Keystatic
services.description: Git-based CMS that stores content as files in your repo — simple, works great for marketing sites and docs

smartMove.tradeoffs: MDX in your code repo is free and simple but makes non-developers dependent on you. A hosted CMS costs money at scale but lets your team move fast. Self-hosted options like Payload save money but mean you're responsible for uptime and security.

service.item: Large images are automatically resized and compressed when uploaded
Content can be previewed exactly as it will appear live before publishing
Required fields are enforced so broken content can't be published
Previous versions of content can be restored if something gets overwritten
Non-technical team members can edit content without filing tickets
Content changes don't require a full site redeploy to go live
There's a clear way to export all your content if you need to switch CMSs
Access controls limit who can publish vs. who can only draft
Content is backed up regularly (either by the CMS or automatically)
Changes to the CMS structure don't break existing published content

diy.item: Images are automatically optimized and served in modern formats (WebP, AVIF)
Content deploys show clear errors if something breaks (not silent failures)
There's version history for content so mistakes can be undone
Non-technical people can edit without knowing markdown, file paths, or git
Content changes trigger fast rebuilds (under 2 minutes) or no rebuild at all
Content is backed up separately from code (not just in git)
Broken content (missing fields, invalid syntax) fails to build with helpful errors
Multiple people can edit content without merge conflicts
Images have reasonable size limits to prevent huge uploads
Preview environment shows exactly what will be published

didYouKnow.stat: The average marketing team makes 4-6 content updates per week. If each one requires developer time to deploy, that's 12-18 hours per month spent being a content bottleneck.

dangerZone.headline: The worst thing that can happen is already happening — you just don't know it yet
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: Your app looks fine on your laptop. But out in the real world, someone on slow WiFi in Brazil is staring at a loading spinner that never ends. Someone else just hit an error that crashed their checkout flow. A third person's data isn't saving. None of them will tell you — they'll just leave. Monitoring isn't about collecting data, it's about knowing which fires to put out first.
dangerZone.failureScenario: Your app launches and gets featured somewhere. Traffic spikes 10x overnight. Your database starts timing out but you don't notice because everything looks fine when you test it. By morning you have 200 frustrated tweets, a 2-star rating forming, and no idea which part of your system is actually broken. You spend hours adding logging and refresh buttons trying to figure it out while more users bounce.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: Immediately after launch when real traffic patterns hit
commonMistakes: Only logging errors on your own computer, not from real users in production
Logging so much that finding the actual problem is like searching for a needle in a haystack
No way to know if the site is down except waiting for someone to email you
Error messages that say "something went wrong" with no context about what or where
Not tracking which errors affect the most users — treating rare glitches the same as showstoppers

service.title: Are you actually seeing the errors users hit?
service.prompt: Check our error tracking setup (Sentry, Highlight, etc). Are errors from production (real users) being captured, or just errors on our development machines? When an error happens, does it include enough context — which user, what page, what they were doing? Are errors grouped so we can see patterns, or is each one treated as a new issue? Can we see a replay of what the user did leading up to the error?
service.whyItMatters: If you only see errors on your laptop, you're missing 99% of what's breaking for real users. And without context, an error message is just a riddle.

service.title: Would you know if your site went down right now?
service.prompt: Check our uptime monitoring (BetterStack, Pingdom, UptimeRobot, etc). Is it checking our site every few minutes from multiple locations? Does it test the actual important pages (login, checkout, dashboard) or just the homepage? When it detects downtime, does someone get a text or call, not just an email? Is there a status page where users can check if it's just them or everyone?
service.whyItMatters: Users expect apps to work 24/7. If your site goes down at 2am and you don't find out until morning, that's hours of lost trust and revenue.

service.title: Can you tell which problems matter most?
service.prompt: Look at how our monitoring tool prioritizes issues. Can we see which errors affect the most users versus rare edge cases? Are we tracking key user actions (signup, checkout, data save) as separate metrics so we know if something critical breaks? Do we get alerts for the big stuff but not for every tiny thing?
service.whyItMatters: Not all errors are created equal. One bug affecting checkout for everyone is more urgent than a typo on a settings page.

service.title: Are you tracking the user experience, not just errors?
service.prompt: Check if we're monitoring performance beyond just crashes. Are we tracking how long pages take to load for real users? Do we see where users get stuck or frustrated? Can we identify slow database queries or API calls? Are we tracking these metrics separately for different browsers, devices, and locations?
service.whyItMatters: Most problems aren't crashes — they're pages that load too slowly or buttons that don't respond fast enough. Users leave before they even see an error.

diy.title: Are errors actually being captured?
diy.prompt: Check our error logging setup. When an error happens in production, where does it go? Is there code that catches errors and saves them somewhere we can review? Are errors on the frontend (browser) and backend (server) both being captured? Do the logs include timestamps, which user it happened to, and what they were trying to do?
diy.whyItMatters: Errors that aren't logged are invisible. If they're only showing in the browser console of a user who already closed the tab, you'll never know it happened.

diy.title: Can you actually find problems in your logs?
diy.prompt: Look at our logging setup. If something goes wrong, can we search logs to find it? Are logs structured (organized data) or just walls of text? Do we log too much (every button click) or too little (only crashes)? Can we filter logs by time, user, or feature? How long do we keep logs before they're deleted?
diy.whyItMatters: Logs are only useful if you can actually find what you're looking for. Too much noise and you'll never spot the signal.

diy.title: Would you know if the site went down?
diy.prompt: Check if we have any automated checks that our site is up and working. Is there something pinging our site every few minutes? Does it test the actual important features (can users log in, can they access their data) or just check if the homepage loads? If it detects a problem, does it alert someone immediately?
diy.whyItMatters: Manual checking doesn't scale. By the time you notice your site is down, dozens or hundreds of users already bounced.

diy.title: Are you measuring what actually matters to users?
diy.prompt: Look at what we're tracking beyond errors. Do we measure page load times? Can we see how long database queries take? Are we tracking when key user actions succeed or fail (signup, save data, checkout)? Can we tell the difference between "slow" and "broken"?
diy.whyItMatters: "It works on my machine" is cold comfort to a user on a phone in a subway. You need to see what they see.

smartMove.reasoning: Basic error logging is easy to build. But connecting errors to specific users, replaying their session to see what happened, grouping similar issues, and getting alerted when something spikes — that's where DIY falls apart. A monitoring service pays for itself the first time it catches a critical bug before you lose a customer.

services.name: Sentry
services.description: Industry standard error tracking with performance monitoring — see exactly what broke and why

services.name: BetterStack
services.description: Uptime monitoring with incident management — get a phone call when your site goes down

services.name: Highlight.io
services.description: Session replay and error tracking in one — watch a video of what users did before hitting an error

smartMove.tradeoffs: Free tiers are generous but fill up fast if you have bugs or traffic. You might need to filter out noisy errors or upgrade sooner than expected. The alternative is finding out about problems from angry users.

service.item: Errors from production (real users) are being captured, not just from your dev environment
You get alerted immediately when your site goes down (text/call, not just email)
Each error shows which user hit it, what page, and what they were doing
Your monitoring checks the actual important pages (login, dashboard, checkout), not just the homepage
High-impact errors (affecting many users) are distinguished from rare edge cases
You can see a recording or replay of what led to an error
Your monitoring service's API keys are only accessible to your backend (not leaked in browser code)
You're tracking page load times and slow queries, not just crashes
Alert noise is under control (you're not ignoring alerts because there are too many)
There's a public status page where users can check if issues are widespread

diy.item: Errors that happen on real users' browsers are logged somewhere you can review
Backend errors include enough context (which endpoint, which user, what data) to debug them
There's a way to search and filter logs (not just scrolling through a giant file)
Something automatically checks if your site is up and alerts you if it's down
Errors include stack traces (the path the code took) so you can find where it broke
You're not logging sensitive data (passwords, credit cards, API keys) in error messages
Logs are retained long enough to investigate issues (at least 30 days)
You can tell which features or pages have the most errors
Page load times and database query speeds are tracked
You have a way to manually trigger an error to test that logging actually works

didYouKnow.stat: The average web app error goes undetected for 4.7 hours when there's no monitoring, and 53% of users will abandon a site that takes more than 3 seconds to load — often without ever reporting a problem.
didYouKnow.source: Google Web Vitals Research & Raygun Error Monitoring Survey 2024

dangerZone.headline: Letting users post things means someone will eventually post something you wish they hadn't
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: A comment box looks simple. Behind it is a sprawling web of questions: who can see what, how to stop spam without blocking real people, what to do when someone posts something illegal or abusive, how to handle deleted comments that other comments replied to, and whether notifications about replies are going to spam your users. Every feature you add (reactions, threading, mentions) multiplies the edge cases.
dangerZone.failureScenario: You launch a community feature for your app. It's going great until someone posts spam links in every thread. You add a basic filter. Spammers adjust. Now they're posting coded messages that look innocent but link to scams in their profiles. Meanwhile, your notification emails are hitting spam folders because you're sending too many. Two months in, you're spending 10 hours a week manually moderating instead of building features.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: 2-6 months before moderation becomes a daily time sink
commonMistakes: No limit on how fast someone can post (so bots flood your app with spam)
Deleted content disappears completely (breaking threads and losing evidence of abuse)
User-uploaded images aren't checked for file type or size (so someone uploads a 50MB executable disguised as a JPEG)
No way to find and remove all content from a banned user at once
Notification emails sent immediately for every action (overwhelming users and triggering spam filters)

service.title: Can your content moderation keep up?
service.prompt: Look at how we handle user-posted content. Is there a way to review flagged content? Can we find all posts by a specific user? Is there rate limiting (like max 10 posts per minute)? If we're using a moderation service like Perspective or OpenAI, does it run on every post or just flagged ones? What happens if the moderation API is slow or down?
service.whyItMatters: Moderation starts small but grows with your user count. What works for 50 users breaks at 500.

service.title: Are you accidentally leaking private content?
service.prompt: Check who can see what. Can users see content from accounts they've blocked? If someone deletes a post, does it disappear from search results and feeds? Are direct messages actually private (not appearing in public APIs or sitemaps)? Can you change the URL to see another user's private content?
service.whyItMatters: The most common bug is hiding content in the UI but forgetting to actually restrict access. It's like closing the curtains but leaving the door unlocked.

service.title: Will user content slow down your app?
service.prompt: Check how content is loaded. Are you loading every single comment at once or paginating (showing 20 at a time)? Are images and videos served through a CDN or directly from your server? Do threads with hundreds of replies take forever to load? Are you counting reactions/likes on every page load or caching the totals?
service.whyItMatters: User content grows unpredictably. The first thread with 500 comments will grind your app to a halt unless you planned for it.

service.title: Do notifications make sense?
service.prompt: Review our notification setup. Can users control what they get notified about? Are notifications batched (like one email with multiple updates) or sent individually? Do reply notifications link directly to the specific comment? Does unsubscribing from one thread stop notifications for that thread only (not everything)?
service.whyItMatters: Bad notifications either overwhelm users (so they ignore everything) or end up in spam folders (so they never arrive).

diy.title: Can bots and spammers be stopped?
diy.prompt: Check our anti-spam measures. Is there rate limiting on posting (like 10 posts per minute max)? Do new accounts have any restrictions? Are links in posts checked against spam lists? Can the same content be posted repeatedly? Is there a captcha or verification for actions like posting or flagging? What stops someone from creating 100 accounts and flooding the system?
diy.whyItMatters: Without rate limiting and basic checks, one determined person with a script can fill your app with garbage in minutes.

diy.title: Is deleted content actually handled correctly?
diy.prompt: Check what happens when content gets deleted. Is it soft-deleted (hidden but kept in the database) or hard-deleted (gone forever)? If someone replies to a comment that later gets deleted, does the reply still make sense? Can moderators see deleted content for investigation? Is there an audit trail of who deleted what and when?
diy.whyItMatters: Hard-deleting content breaks conversation threads and removes evidence if you need to investigate abuse or respond to legal requests.

diy.title: Are file uploads a security risk?
diy.prompt: Look at how we handle user-uploaded files (images, videos, documents). Are file types restricted (like images only, no executables)? Is there a size limit? Are filenames sanitized to prevent injection attacks? Are files scanned before being served to other users? Are upload URLs rate-limited so someone can't upload thousands of files?
diy.whyItMatters: File uploads are a classic attack vector. Someone uploads a malicious file disguised as an image, and suddenly your app is serving malware.

diy.title: Will user content scale?
diy.prompt: Check how content queries are structured. Are you loading all comments in one database query or paginating? Do queries filter by user permissions (so private content doesn't leak)? Are there database indexes on commonly searched fields like user ID and timestamp? What happens if someone posts a 10,000-character comment?
diy.whyItMatters: The query that works fine for 100 comments takes 30 seconds to load when there are 10,000. User content grows faster than you expect.

smartMove.reasoning: Basic user content (like comments on your own posts) is reasonable to build yourself. But if you're adding reactions, threading, real-time updates, or full social features (feeds, follows, mentions), the complexity explodes fast. And moderation is always harder than you think.

smartMove.tradeoffs: Building it yourself means full control and no recurring costs, but moderation becomes your problem. Services handle the hard parts but add monthly fees and you're trusting them with your user data.

didYouKnow.stat: The average social platform spends $2.50-$3.50 per user per year on content moderation, and 60% of users abandon platforms where they encounter spam or abuse.

dangerZone.headline: Bad support doesn't just lose one customer — it loses everyone they tell about their experience
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: A contact form seems fine until you get your 20th email and realize: there's no way to know which you've already answered, two people replied to the same person with different answers, someone's follow-up went to a different inbox, and you've lost track of who's still waiting. Support scales badly if you don't plan for it.
dangerZone.failureScenario: You launch and get traction. Support emails flood in. Your co-founder answers some, you answer others, replies get buried in different inboxes. A customer emails three times over two weeks with no response because their messages went to a folder no one checks. They post about it publicly. Now you're scrambling to apologize while looking through 400 emails to find everyone else you've accidentally ignored.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: 3-6 months once you start getting real traction
commonMistakes: Multiple people answering the same ticket without knowing someone else already replied
No way to see what you've already told a customer in previous conversations
Support emails mixed with everything else in a personal inbox
No shared access — if the person who knows the answer is on vacation, everyone waits
Missing urgent issues because they look like regular emails in a crowded inbox

service.title: Can you actually find and track conversations?
service.prompt: Look at our support setup. When someone emails twice about the same problem, can we see their history? If two team members are working at the same time, can they see what the other is handling? Can we tell which messages still need a reply? Is there a way to mark something as urgent? Can we see how long people typically wait for a response?
service.whyItMatters: Once you pass about 20 conversations, trying to track support in regular email becomes chaos. You'll forget people, duplicate work, and lose track of what you promised.

service.title: Does your chat widget slow down your site?
service.prompt: Check how our chat widget affects site performance. How much does it add to page load time? Does it load on every page (even ones where chat makes no sense, like checkout)? Is it pulling in a lot of extra code? Does the page wait for the chat to load before showing content?
service.whyItMatters: Chat widgets add third-party code to every page. A heavy one can add 1-2 seconds to load time, killing your conversion rate just to offer support most people never use.

service.title: Are customers finding answers before messaging you?
service.prompt: Review what happens before someone contacts support. Do we have a help center or FAQ? When someone opens the chat widget, do they see helpful articles first? Are common questions answered somewhere visible? Can we see which articles people actually read versus which get ignored?
service.whyItMatters: Every support message takes human time. The best support systems answer common questions automatically, so you only spend time on things that actually need a person.

service.title: Is customer data shared with your support tool safely?
service.prompt: Check what information our support tool can see. Are we sending sensitive data (payment info, passwords) where support staff can see it? Is the connection between our app and the support tool encrypted? Can support staff see more customer data than they need to? Are API keys stored securely?
service.whyItMatters: Support tools often get access to customer names, emails, and usage data. If that connection isn't locked down, it's another way for data to leak.

diy.title: Can you track who said what?
diy.prompt: Look at how we're handling support messages. Can we see the full history of conversations with each customer? When someone emails back, does it connect to their previous message? If two people are answering messages, can they see what the other person already said? Is there a way to mark messages as answered or still-waiting?
diy.whyItMatters: Without conversation tracking, you'll tell customers contradictory things, forget what you promised, and make people repeat themselves.

diy.title: Are support emails getting lost?
diy.prompt: Check where support messages go. Do all support emails go to one place everyone can see? What happens if someone replies and their message goes to spam? Is there a backup if emails don't arrive? Can you tell if an email was delivered and read? Are bounced or rejected emails captured somewhere?
diy.whyItMatters: Email delivery is unreliable. If your only support channel is email and you don't know when messages get lost, customers will think you're ignoring them.

diy.title: Can customers see their own support history?
diy.prompt: Check whether customers can look up their previous conversations. When they email back about the same issue, do they have to explain everything again? Can they see what you told them last time? Is there any way for them to check the status of their issue?
diy.whyItMatters: Making customers repeat themselves is frustrating. Being able to reference past conversations makes everyone's life easier.

diy.title: What happens when support volume spikes?
diy.prompt: Think through what happens if support messages suddenly triple. Will they all pile up in one person's inbox? Is there a way to distribute them across a team? Can you set up auto-replies to manage expectations? Is there any priority system for urgent issues?
diy.whyItMatters: Support volume is unpredictable. A bug, a launch spike, or a viral moment can flood you with messages faster than you can answer.

smartMove.reasoning: Early on, a contact form and shared email work fine. But once you're handling 30+ conversations a month or have multiple people doing support, the time you waste juggling emails costs more than a tool. If you're charging customers, especially for subscriptions, professional support tools pay for themselves immediately.

didYouKnow.stat: 70% of customers will do business with a company again after a problem is resolved quickly — but 58% will never use a company again after one bad support experience.

dangerZone.headline: A messy CRM doesn't crash — it just quietly makes your sales team slower until nobody uses it
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: A CRM looks like a fancy contact list with some extra fields. But it's actually the system that decides how your entire sales process works: who follows up with who, what information matters at what stage, how deals move through your pipeline, and what gets reported to leadership. Build it wrong and it becomes a data graveyard where leads go to die — build it right and it runs your whole revenue operation.
dangerZone.failureScenario: You build a custom CRM because "we're just tracking contacts and deals, how hard can it be?" Six months later you have 1,000 contacts, half with duplicate entries, a third with missing information, and nobody can answer "how many deals are closing this month?" because everyone tracks things differently. Your sales team goes back to their personal spreadsheets because your system is too slow and too confusing.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: 3-6 months before data quality degrades to uselessness
commonMistakes: No deduplication — same customer exists 5 times with slightly different emails
Activity history scattered across different tables making it impossible to see conversation threads
Search that's too slow or too limited — finding a contact takes 30 seconds
No clear ownership rules — multiple people follow up with the same lead
Deal values and stages that don't match what actually closed, making forecasting worthless

service.title: Is your CRM actually being used?
service.prompt: Check how our team is using the CRM. Are all sales activities (calls, emails, meetings) getting logged consistently? Are contact records complete or full of empty fields? When someone leaves the company, does their data stay accessible? Is there a clear process for when a lead becomes an opportunity becomes a customer? Can everyone find what they need in under 10 seconds?
service.whyItMatters: A CRM that people don't use is worse than no CRM at all — it creates the illusion of organization while actual information lives in people's heads and private notes.

service.title: Can you trust your pipeline numbers?
service.prompt: Look at how deals move through stages in our CRM. Do stage definitions match how we actually sell? Are deal values accurate or just guesses? Can you see when a deal last had activity? If you sum up all "likely to close this month" deals, does that number match what actually closes? Are there deals stuck in stages for months?
service.whyItMatters: Sales forecasting only works if the data is honest. If your CRM shows $100K closing this month but reality is $20K, you can't plan anything.

service.title: Is customer data syncing correctly?
service.prompt: Check how our CRM connects to other tools. If someone pays through Stripe, does that update in the CRM? If support handles a ticket in Zendesk, does the sales team see it? Are email opens and clicks tracking correctly? When data flows between systems, is it creating duplicates or losing information?
service.whyItMatters: A CRM that doesn't talk to your other tools becomes a second source of truth — which means nobody trusts it.

service.title: Can your CRM handle your growth?
service.prompt: Look at performance as data grows. How quickly can you search across all contacts? How long does it take to load a contact with a long activity history? Are there limits on custom fields, automation rules, or the number of records? What happens if you import 10,000 contacts at once?
service.whyItMatters: A CRM that slows down as you grow means your sales process gets slower just when you need it to speed up.

diy.title: Can people actually find what they need?
diy.prompt: Test our CRM's search and filtering. Can you search by partial name, email, company, or phone number? Does it handle typos reasonably? Can you filter by deal stage, contact source, last activity date, and custom fields? How long does each search take? Is there a way to save common searches?
diy.whyItMatters: If finding a contact takes more than a few seconds, people will maintain their own spreadsheets instead of using your CRM.

diy.title: Is duplicate data being prevented?
diy.prompt: Check our deduplication logic. What happens if someone enters a contact that already exists? Does the system catch it and merge, or create a duplicate? Can it detect duplicates even with slightly different emails or name spellings? Is there a way to bulk-find and merge duplicates? What happens when two people edit the same record at the same time?
diy.whyItMatters: Duplicate contacts create confusion, wasted outreach, and embarrassing situations where you pitch the same person twice.

diy.title: Is activity history actually useful?
diy.prompt: Look at how activities (calls, emails, meetings, notes) are stored and displayed. Can you see a complete timeline of interactions with a contact? Are activities linked to both contacts and deals? Can you see who on your team did what and when? Is there a way to set reminders or see overdue follow-ups?
diy.whyItMatters: A contact record without history is just a business card. The value is knowing what you talked about last time and what needs to happen next.

diy.title: Will your CRM survive real usage?
diy.prompt: Stress test the basic operations. What happens if 10 team members are using it simultaneously? How long does it take to load a contact with 100+ activities? Can you bulk-import 1,000 contacts without breaking things? Does exporting data work reliably? What happens if someone deletes something by accident?
diy.whyItMatters: CRMs get slower as they fill up. If yours can't handle growth, you'll be rebuilding it right when you're busiest.

smartMove.reasoning: Here's the truth: a spreadsheet actually works fine until you have a real sales team. Once you need deal stages, activity tracking, and more than 3 people managing relationships, a proper CRM pays for itself immediately. The free tiers are generous enough that building your own only makes sense if you have genuinely weird requirements.

services.name: HubSpot

services.name: Pipedrive

services.name: Folk

smartMove.tradeoffs: Using a service means learning their way of doing things and paying per user as you grow. Building your own means you can customize everything but you're also responsible for every edge case and performance issue. Most teams underestimate how complex a good CRM actually is.

Contact ownership is clear — no confusion about who's following up
Pipeline reports show accurate numbers that match reality
Mobile access works if your team needs to update on the go

Search results appear in under 2 seconds even with thousands of contacts

didYouKnow.stat: Sales teams spend an average of 17% of their time on data entry and CRM updates — that's nearly a full day per week not spent actually selling.

dangerZone.headline: A double-booked appointment doesn't just waste time — it destroys trust in your entire platform
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: A booking form looks simple — pick a date, pick a time, click confirm. But behind that form is a nightmare of edge cases: what happens when two people book the same slot at the same millisecond, how recurring appointments interact with holidays and DST changes, how cancellation windows and no-show policies work, timezone conversion when the provider is in New York but the customer is in Tokyo, and buffer time between back-to-back appointments. Every calendar app looks easy until real humans start using it across real timezones.
dangerZone.failureScenario: You launch a tutoring marketplace. Bookings work great for the first month. Then Daylight Saving Time hits and every appointment in the affected timezone shifts by an hour. Half your tutors have back-to-back sessions with no break, the other half have phantom gaps. Parents are showing up an hour early or an hour late. You spend a week manually fixing hundreds of bookings while refunding angry customers.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: 1-3 months before timezone or concurrency issues surface
commonMistakes: Storing times without timezone info — everything works until your first user is in a different timezone
No safeguard to prevent two people from booking the same slot at the exact same moment
Recurring events that don't account for Daylight Saving Time, holidays, or provider schedule changes
Cancellation and refund logic that doesn't handle edge cases like partial refunds or last-minute cancellations
No buffer time between appointments — back-to-back bookings with zero transition time

service.title: How does your booking service handle concurrent reservations?
service.prompt: Check how the scheduling service (like Calendly, Cal.com, or Acuity) prevents double-bookings. If two users try to book the same slot at the exact same time, what happens? Does it use a first-come-first-served approach to prevent conflicts? Open two browser tabs and try to book the same slot simultaneously — does one get rejected cleanly?
service.whyItMatters: Double bookings are the fastest way to lose trust. If your service doesn't handle race conditions, you'll find out from angry users, not from testing.

service.title: Are timezones handled correctly end-to-end?
service.prompt: Check how times are stored and displayed. Does the booking service store times in a universal standard format (like UTC) and convert them for each user's timezone? If a provider in London sets availability for 2pm, does a user in New York correctly see it as 9am? What happens around Daylight Saving Time transitions — do bookings shift by an hour or stay correct?
service.whyItMatters: Timezone bugs are silent — everything looks fine until DST changes or you get your first international user. Then every time displayed in the app could be wrong.

service.title: What happens when someone cancels or reschedules?
service.prompt: Walk through the cancellation and rescheduling flow. Is there a cancellation window after which refunds aren't available? When someone reschedules, does the original slot immediately become available again? Are both parties notified? Can cancellation be abused (book and cancel repeatedly to block someone's calendar)?
service.whyItMatters: Cancellation logic sounds simple but touches payments, notifications, availability, and policy enforcement all at once. Getting any piece wrong creates confusion or lost revenue.

service.title: Do reminders and notifications fire reliably?
service.prompt: Check the reminder system. Are email/SMS reminders sent at the right times relative to the appointment? What timezone are the reminder times calculated in? If a user reschedules, do the old reminders get cancelled and new ones created? What happens if the notification service is temporarily down — do reminders get retried or silently lost?
service.whyItMatters: No-shows often happen because reminders failed. A missed reminder means a wasted slot, lost revenue, and a frustrated provider.

diy.title: Is your time slot logic race-condition safe?
diy.prompt: Look at how available time slots are calculated and reserved. When a user selects a slot, is it locked or held before they complete the booking? If two users submit at the same time, does the database enforce uniqueness (not just the application code)? Show me the database constraint or transaction isolation level that prevents double-bookings.
diy.whyItMatters: Application-level checks alone aren't enough — under load, two requests can pass the 'is available' check simultaneously. Only database-level constraints guarantee no double-bookings.

diy.title: How are dates and times stored?
diy.prompt: Check the database schema for appointment/booking records. Are times stored in a universal format (UTC) with a separate timezone field? When displaying times to users, is the conversion happening on the server or client? What happens if a user's browser timezone doesn't match their profile timezone? Search for any use of new Date() without explicit timezone handling.
diy.whyItMatters: Storing times in the user's local format instead of a universal standard is a ticking bomb. It works until you have users in multiple timezones or DST changes, then every displayed time could be an hour off.

diy.title: How do recurring appointments work?
diy.prompt: If you support recurring bookings, check how they're generated. Are future occurrences pre-created in the database or calculated on the fly? What happens when a recurring appointment falls on a holiday or when the provider marks that day as unavailable? Can individual occurrences be modified without affecting the series? How far into the future are occurrences generated?
diy.whyItMatters: Recurring events are one of the hardest problems in scheduling. Google Calendar has been working on it for 20 years and still has edge cases. Building it from scratch means inheriting all those edge cases.

diy.title: Can your scheduling handle load?
diy.prompt: Check how availability is calculated. Does every request to view available slots query the entire bookings table? Is there caching for provider availability windows? If a popular provider has 1,000 people viewing their calendar simultaneously, how many database queries does that generate? Are availability calculations indexed properly?
diy.whyItMatters: Availability calculations run on every page view of a booking page. Without proper indexing and caching, a popular provider's page becomes unusably slow.

smartMove.reasoning: Scheduling looks deceptively simple but hides enormous complexity in timezone handling, concurrency, recurring events, and calendar sync. Services like Cal.com or Calendly have spent years solving these edge cases. Unless scheduling is your core product differentiator, use a service and focus on what makes your app unique.

services.name: Acuity Scheduling

smartMove.tradeoffs: Scheduling services handle the hard parts (timezone math, calendar sync, conflict resolution) but you're limited to their booking flows and UI patterns. Deep customization often requires their highest pricing tiers or building around their API limitations.

dangerZone.headline: A wrong pin on the map doesn't just look bad — it sends real people to the wrong place
dangerZone.hiddenComplexity: Dropping a pin on a map looks like one API call. But behind that pin is a world of complexity: geocoding addresses into coordinates (and handling the ones that don't match), calculating accurate distances and ETAs that account for traffic and road type, displaying maps that load fast on slow connections, handling GPS drift and inaccurate location data from phones, managing API rate limits and costs that scale with every map view, and dealing with addresses that exist in the real world but not in any geocoding database.
dangerZone.failureScenario: You launch a delivery app. Orders roll in. Then you discover that your geocoding service interprets '123 Main St' differently in every city — some deliveries go to the right address in the wrong city. Drivers are calling customers to figure out where they actually are. Your ETA estimates assume straight-line distance, so a 5-minute delivery across a river with no bridge becomes a 30-minute detour. Customers see '5 min away' while their food gets cold.
dangerZone.timeToBreak: 2-4 weeks after launch in a new geographic area
commonMistakes: Using straight-line distance ('as the crow flies') instead of actual driving/walking distance
Not handling address ambiguity — '123 Main St' exists in thousands of cities and the app picks one without asking
Ignoring how costs scale — every map view and address lookup costs money, and it adds up fast with real traffic
Trusting the location your users' phones report as exact — it can be off by 50+ meters in cities with tall buildings
Relying on a single map provider with no backup plan — one outage takes down your entire location feature

service.title: How much are your map API calls actually costing?
service.prompt: Check your map provider's (Google Maps, Mapbox, etc.) billing dashboard. How many API calls are you making per user session? Are map tiles being cached or re-fetched on every page load? Is geocoding happening client-side (billable per request) when it could be cached server-side? Calculate the cost per active user per month at your current usage pattern and project it to 10x users.
service.whyItMatters: Map APIs charge per request and costs grow linearly with traffic. A single page that loads a map, geocodes an address, and calculates a route can cost $0.02-0.05 per view. At 10,000 daily users, that's $200-500/month just for maps.

service.title: How does your app handle bad or ambiguous addresses?
service.prompt: Test your geocoding with tricky inputs: an address that exists in multiple cities, a misspelled street name, a PO Box, an apartment with a unit number, and an address in a rural area with no street number. Does your app show the user which location was matched so they can confirm? What happens when geocoding returns zero results — does the user see a helpful error or a broken map?
service.whyItMatters: Real-world addresses are messy. Roughly 10-15% of addresses won't geocode cleanly on the first try. If your app silently picks the wrong match, users end up at the wrong location with no idea why.

service.title: Do your maps work well on mobile and slow connections?
service.prompt: Test your map on a real phone with a throttled connection (3G speed). How long does the map take to become interactive? Are you loading the full map SDK on pages that only need a static map image? Do map tiles load progressively or does the user stare at a grey box? Is the map touch-friendly — can users pinch to zoom and drag to pan without accidentally triggering other UI elements?
service.whyItMatters: Maps are the heaviest assets on most pages. The Google Maps JavaScript SDK alone is 200KB+. On mobile with a slow connection, an unoptimized map can make your entire page feel broken.

service.title: Is user location data handled safely?
service.prompt: Check what location data you're storing about users. Are you logging exact GPS coordinates with timestamps (which creates a movement history)? Is location data encrypted at rest? Do you have a retention policy — are you keeping location history forever? If a user deletes their account, is their location history actually deleted? Does your privacy policy mention location tracking?
service.whyItMatters: Location data is among the most sensitive personal information. A leaked database with timestamped GPS coordinates reveals where every user lives, works, and goes — creating serious safety and legal liability.

diy.title: Are your distance and ETA calculations accurate?
diy.prompt: Check how your app calculates distances between points. Are you using straight-line distance ('as the crow flies') or actual road-based routing? If you're showing ETAs, do they account for the mode of transport (driving vs walking vs cycling)? Test a route that requires crossing a river, highway, or one-way street system — does the estimate match reality? Are you caching route calculations or hitting the API on every request?
diy.whyItMatters: Straight-line distance can be 2-5x shorter than actual travel distance in cities with rivers, highways, or one-way streets. Showing inaccurate ETAs destroys user trust fast.

diy.title: How do you handle GPS accuracy and drift?
diy.prompt: Check how your app processes location data from device GPS. Are you filtering out obviously wrong coordinates (like sudden jumps of hundreds of meters)? Do you show accuracy radius to users or just a dot? In areas with poor GPS (urban canyons, indoors), does your app gracefully degrade or show a wildly bouncing marker? If you're tracking movement (delivery, ride-sharing), how do you smooth the path?
diy.whyItMatters: Phone GPS can be off by 5-50 meters depending on environment. In downtown areas with tall buildings, it can jump erratically. Without smoothing and accuracy handling, your location features look unreliable.

diy.title: Can your map infrastructure handle scale?
diy.prompt: Check how map data flows through your system. Are map tiles served from your own server or a CDN? How many markers can your map render before it starts lagging — test with 100, 1000, and 10,000 points? Are you using marker clustering for dense areas? If you're rendering custom map overlays, do they update efficiently when the user pans and zooms?
diy.whyItMatters: Maps with thousands of markers will freeze the browser. Without clustering, zoom-level filtering, or viewport-based loading, your map becomes unusable as your data grows.

diy.title: What happens when the map API is down or rate-limited?
diy.prompt: Check your app's behavior when the map provider is unavailable. Temporarily block the map API domain and see what happens — does the page crash, show a blank area, or degrade gracefully? Do you have a fallback for geocoding if the primary provider hits rate limits? Are API keys stored securely and rotated, or hardcoded in client-side code where anyone can steal them?
diy.whyItMatters: Map APIs have outages and rate limits. If your entire app depends on one provider with no fallback, a 30-minute API outage means 30 minutes of a broken app for all your users.

smartMove.reasoning: If you just need to show a map with pins and basic geocoding, a service like Google Maps or Mapbox with their SDKs is the clear choice. But if location is your core product (delivery routing, fleet tracking, real estate search), you'll likely need a combination of services with custom logic on top. The key decision is how much location intelligence lives in your code vs. in API calls.

Mapbox offers better pricing and customization but has weaker coverage in some regions. Switching providers later means rewriting all your map UI and location logic.
